questions.text: A client is placed on a clear liquid diet following bowel surgery. Which food item is appropriate for the nurse to offer?

options.A: Orange juice
options.B: Vanilla ice cream
options.C: Cola soda
options.D: Milk with coffee

questions.rationale: Cola soda is a dark carbonated beverage explicitly allowed on a clear liquid diet. Orange juice has pulp (not allowed), ice cream contains milk/fat (not allowed), and milk in coffee is not allowed.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with GERD. Which statement by the client indicates correct understanding?

options.A: "I should lie down for 30 minutes after eating to rest my stomach."
options.B: "I can eat large meals as long as I avoid spicy foods."
options.C: "I should not eat anything for at least 3 hours before going to bed."
options.D: "Peppermint tea is a safe alternative to coffee."

questions.rationale: Eating within 3 hours of bedtime promotes reflux when lying flat. Lying down after eating worsens GERD (A is wrong). Large meals worsen GERD (B is wrong). Peppermint relaxes the LES and worsens GERD (D is wrong).

questions.text: A client presents with complaints of burning epigastric pain that improves after eating. The nurse recognizes this pattern as most consistent with which condition?

options.A: Gastric ulcer
options.B: Duodenal ulcer
options.C: Crohn's disease
options.D: Cholecystitis

questions.rationale: Duodenal ulcers characteristically improve with eating because food temporarily buffers stomach acid. Gastric ulcer pain often worsens with eating.

questions.text: The nurse is assessing a client suspected of having appendicitis. Which sequence of symptoms is most consistent with this diagnosis?

options.A: Nausea and vomiting followed by abdominal pain
options.B: Abdominal pain followed by nausea and vomiting
options.C: Fever followed by diarrhea and cramping
options.D: Bloody stools followed by abdominal pain

questions.rationale: In appendicitis, abdominal pain comes FIRST, then nausea/vomiting follow. In gastroenteritis, nausea/vomiting come first. This distinction is a critical NCLEX concept.

questions.text: A client with peritonitis is being positioned in bed. Which position should the nurse place the client in?

options.A: Prone
options.B: Trendelenburg
options.C: Semi-Fowler's
options.D: Supine with legs elevated

questions.rationale: Semi-Fowler's position promotes drainage of peritoneal fluid to the pelvis and facilitates lung expansion in peritonitis.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with shingles. Which isolation precautions are required until the lesions are fully crusted over?

options.A: Contact precautions only
options.B: Droplet precautions only
options.C: Airborne and Contact precautions
options.D: Standard precautions only

questions.rationale: Shingles requires both Airborne AND Contact precautions until ALL lesions are crusted over, because the vesicular fluid contains active virus.

questions.text: A client is receiving Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN). The nurse understands that TPN must be administered through which type of access?

options.A: Peripheral IV in the forearm
options.B: Central venous line
options.C: Nasogastric tube
options.D: Subcutaneous infusion port

questions.rationale: TPN is a hypertonic solution that must be administered through a central venous line. Peripheral veins cannot tolerate the high osmolarity and would cause phlebitis.

questions.text: The nurse is preparing to administer acyclovir for a client diagnosed with shingles. Which assessment finding would cause the nurse to question the effectiveness of treatment?

options.A: The rash appeared 5 days ago and antiviral therapy has not yet been initiated
options.B: The client reports moderate pain at the rash site
options.C: The client's lesions are beginning to crust over
options.D: The client has a history of chickenpox as a child

questions.rationale: Antiviral medications (acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir) must be initiated within 72 hours of rash onset to be effective. A 5-day delay means the treatment window has passed.

questions.text: Which of the following clients is at highest risk for developing cholecystitis? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A 45-year-old obese female
options.B: A 22-year-old athletic male
options.C: A pregnant woman
options.D: A client on hormone replacement therapy
options.E: A client with Crohn's disease

questions.rationale: Risk factors: Female, obese, pregnant, on hormone replacement therapy, and Crohn's disease (disrupts bile acid reabsorption). An athletic young male has the fewest risk factors.

questions.text: A client is started on finasteride for BPH. The client asks how long it will take to notice improvement. The nurse's best response is:

options.A: "You should notice improvement within 1–2 days."
options.B: "Improvement typically occurs within 1–2 weeks."
options.C: "It takes approximately 3–6 months to see the full effect."
options.D: "The medication will only work if combined with a catheter."

questions.rationale: Finasteride (a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor) takes 3–6 months to shrink the prostate and produce noticeable improvement because the prostate does not shrink overnight.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with ulcerative colitis. Which statement correctly describes the pattern of disease?

options.A: Inflammation begins in the cecum and moves down to the rectum
options.B: Inflammation begins in the rectum and moves continuously toward the cecum
options.C: Ulcerations are patchy with areas of normal tissue in between
options.D: The disease involves all layers of the bowel wall

questions.rationale: UC begins in the rectum and moves continuously toward the cecum (no skip lesions). It is limited to the mucosa (not transmural like Crohn's).

questions.text: Which lab value would the nurse most expect to find in a client with peritonitis?

options.A: WBC 5,000/mm³
options.B: WBC 8,500/mm³
options.C: WBC 20,000/mm³ or higher
options.D: WBC 12,000/mm³

questions.rationale: WBC of 20,000/mm³ or higher is the expected finding in peritonitis, indicating significant infectious/inflammatory response.

questions.text: The nurse is educating a client about shingles transmission. Which statement by the client demonstrates correct understanding?

options.A: "I can give someone else shingles if they are around me."
options.B: "I can give chickenpox to someone who has never had chickenpox or been vaccinated."
options.C: "Shingles can only spread by direct contact with my blood."
options.D: "The shingles virus cannot spread once I start antiviral medication."

questions.rationale: You cannot give someone shingles, but you CAN transmit chickenpox to someone who has never had chickenpox or been vaccinated, through contact with the active lesions.

questions.text: A client with meningitis asks why they need a lumbar puncture. The nurse explains that the purpose is to:

options.A: Relieve intracranial pressure immediately
options.B: Administer antibiotics directly into the spinal fluid
options.C: Obtain a sample of cerebrospinal fluid for analysis and culture
options.D: Measure the client's intracranial pressure accurately

questions.rationale: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) obtains CSF for culture, sensitivity, and analysis — it is the definitive diagnostic test for meningitis.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a post-operative client following a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The client complains of right shoulder pain. The nurse's best response is:

options.A: Notify the provider immediately — this may indicate internal bleeding
options.B: Encourage ambulation and apply warmth — this is expected from the CO₂ gas
options.C: Place the client in the Trendelenburg position to relieve the pressure
options.D: Administer oxygen and obtain an ECG to rule out cardiac causes

questions.rationale: Referred shoulder pain after laparoscopic cholecystectomy is caused by CO₂ gas rising and irritating the diaphragm. Ambulation and warmth help resolve this expected discomfort.

questions.text: A client with gastroenteritis is experiencing profuse diarrhea. Which electrolyte imbalance does the nurse prioritize monitoring for?

options.A: Hypernatremia
options.B: Hypercalcemia
options.C: Hypokalemia
options.D: Hyperphosphatemia

questions.rationale: Potassium (K⁺) is lost in diarrhea, making hypokalemia the primary electrolyte concern in gastroenteritis. Hypokalemia can cause muscle weakness and cardiac dysrhythmias.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most accurate test for diagnosing GERD?

options.A: EGD (Esophagogastroduodenoscopy)
options.B: Ambulatory pH monitoring with esophageal capsule
options.C: Barium swallow
options.D: Abdominal ultrasound

questions.rationale: Ambulatory pH monitoring (capsule affixed to distal esophagus) is the most accurate diagnostic test for GERD. EGD is used to visualize tissue damage but does not measure acid exposure.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with a bowel obstruction. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.A: Ambulate the client in the hallway twice per day
options.B: Insert and maintain a nasogastric tube for decompression
options.C: Administer stool softeners as ordered
options.D: Encourage oral fluids to prevent dehydration

questions.rationale: Inserting and maintaining an NG tube for gastric decompression is the priority nursing intervention for bowel obstruction — it relieves pressure and prevents further complications.

questions.text: Crohn's disease most commonly affects which part of the gastrointestinal tract?

options.A: Rectum
options.B: Cecum
options.C: Terminal ileum
options.D: Duodenum

questions.rationale: Crohn's disease most commonly affects the terminal ileum, though it can affect any part of the GI tract from mouth to anus.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease develops a fistula. The nurse understands that the client is now at highest risk for which serious complications? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Pancreatitis
options.B: Intraabdominal abscess
options.C: Peritonitis
options.D: Sepsis
options.E: Hepatitis

questions.rationale: Fistulas in Crohn's disease put clients at high risk for intraabdominal abscess, peritonitis, and sepsis. Hepatitis and pancreatitis are not specifically listed as direct fistula complications.

questions.text: The nurse is about to administer the first dose of prazosin to a client with BPH. What is the most important timing consideration?

options.A: Give it with breakfast
options.B: Give it before the client's morning shower
options.C: Give it at bedtime
options.D: Give it with an antacid to improve absorption

questions.rationale: Prazosin must be given at bedtime to prevent first-dose syncope. The initial drop in blood pressure is best tolerated while lying down.

questions.text: Which type of peptic ulcer is directly associated with extensive burns?

options.A: Cushing's ulcer
options.B: Ischemic ulcer
options.C: Gastric ulcer
options.D: Curling's ulcer

questions.rationale: Curling's ulcer is a stress ulcer specifically associated with extensive burns (loss of protective GI mucus from the physiologic stress of burns). Cushing's ulcer = increased ICP.

questions.text: A client has been diagnosed with bacterial meningitis. Which type of isolation precautions should the nurse implement?

options.A: Airborne precautions
options.B: Contact precautions
options.C: Droplet precautions
options.D: Standard precautions only

questions.rationale: Bacterial meningitis requires droplet precautions (bacteria spread via respiratory droplets). Viral meningitis requires only standard precautions.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client's chart and finds that TPN was inadvertently stopped by the previous nurse. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.A: Restart the TPN immediately at the original rate
options.B: Infuse D10W (10% dextrose in water) until TPN is available
options.C: Administer orange juice orally to prevent hypoglycemia
options.D: Notify the provider and wait for new orders before doing anything

questions.rationale: If TPN is unavailable, infuse D10W at the same rate to prevent hypoglycemia. The pancreas is still producing insulin for the high glucose load from TPN, so abrupt discontinuation causes a dangerous drop in blood sugar.

questions.text: Which dietary modification is most appropriate for a client with cholecystitis?

options.A: High fat diet to stimulate bile production
options.B: Low fat, cholesterol-restricted diet
options.C: High fiber diet to improve bowel motility
options.D: Renal diet to protect kidney function

questions.rationale: Low fat, cholesterol-restricted diet is appropriate for cholecystitis because fat triggers gallbladder contractions, which worsen pain and inflammation.

questions.text: The nurse is assessing a client's shingles rash. Which characteristic is expected with herpes zoster?

options.A: The rash appears bilaterally across the chest
options.B: The rash crosses the midline of the body
options.C: The rash is grouped and follows a single dermatome pathway on one side
options.D: The rash begins on the face and spreads to the trunk

questions.rationale: Shingles rash is always grouped, unilateral (one side), and follows a specific dermatome pathway. It never crosses the midline.

questions.text: A client is receiving enteral nutrition. The nurse is preparing to administer medications. Which action is correct?

options.A: Mix the medications directly into the feeding formula
options.B: Stop the tube feeding before administering medications
options.C: Continue the feeding and flush with 5 mL of saline before each medication
options.D: Medications do not need any special consideration with enteral feedings

questions.rationale: Tube feedings must be stopped before medication administration to prevent drugformula interactions and ensure proper drug absorption.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client post-TURP. The client reports a constant urge to urinate. The nurse's best response is:

options.A: "This may indicate a urinary tract infection — I'll notify the provider."
options.B: "This is expected because the catheter is large and presses on stretch receptors."
options.C: "Try not to urinate around the catheter, it will clear up on its own."
options.D: Both B and C

questions.rationale: Both B and C are correct. The constant urge to void is normal (large catheter presses on stretch receptors), AND the patient should not try to urinate around the catheter (causes bladder spasms).

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis is at risk for which life-threatening complication involving massive colon dilation?

options.A: Pyloric obstruction
options.B: Toxic megacolon
options.C: Intussusception
options.D: Volvulus

questions.rationale: Toxic megacolon is the most dangerous complication of ulcerative colitis — massive colon dilation leads to ileus, gangrene, and sepsis if untreated.

questions.text: A client with meningitis is receiving mannitol. The nurse understands the purpose of this medication is to:

options.A: Prevent seizures
options.B: Treat bacterial infection
options.C: Reduce intracranial pressure
options.D: Prevent deep vein thrombosis

questions.rationale: Mannitol is a hyperosmolar agent that pulls fluid from brain tissue into the bloodstream, reducing intracranial pressure.

questions.text: Which of the following is the classic triad of signs associated with meningitis?

options.A: Fever, rash, joint pain
options.B: Severe headache, stiff neck, fever
options.C: Nausea, vomiting, photophobia
options.D: Confusion, hypotension, tachycardia

questions.rationale: The classic triad of meningitis is severe headache + stiff neck (nuchal rigidity) + fever.

questions.text: A client diagnosed with GERD asks the nurse which foods to avoid. The nurse's teaching should include which items? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Chocolate
options.B: Oatmeal
options.C: Peppermint
options.D: Orange juice
options.E: Carbonated beverages

questions.rationale: All these foods trigger GERD by relaxing the LES or increasing acid. Oatmeal is generally a safe, non-irritating food.

questions.text: What is the primary cause of bowel obstruction in clients who have had previous abdominal surgery?

options.A: Tumors
options.B: Volvulus
options.C: Adhesions (scar tissue)
options.D: Intussusception

questions.rationale: Adhesions (scar tissue from previous surgery) are the most common cause of mechanical bowel obstruction.

questions.text: A client has been started on mesalamine for ulcerative colitis. Which instruction should the nurse include in the teaching?

options.A: Crush the tablet and mix with applesauce for easier swallowing
options.B: Take the medication only during flare-ups
options.C: Do not crush the extended-release tablet
options.D: Expect relief of symptoms within 24 hours

questions.rationale: Extended-release mesalamine must never be crushed — crushing destroys the extended-release mechanism, causing too much drug to be released at once and potentially causing toxicity.

questions.text: The nurse is assessing a post-operative appendectomy client. Which finding requires immediate intervention?

options.A: Pain rating of 4/10 at the incision site
options.B: Temperature of 100.1°F (37.8°C) on postoperative day 1
options.C: Sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain with a rigid abdomen
options.D: Fatigue and reluctance to ambulate

questions.rationale: Sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain with a rigid abdomen in a post-appendectomy client signals peritonitis — an acute abdominal emergency requiring immediate intervention.

questions.text: Which medication used for BPH requires PSA level monitoring, and why?

options.A: Prazosin — because it affects kidney function
options.B: Finasteride — because it lowers PSA levels by approximately 50%, which could mask prostate cancer
options.C: Mesalamine — because it affects colon inflammation markers
options.D: Loperamide — because it can cause electrolyte disturbances

questions.rationale: Finasteride reduces PSA levels by ~50%, so a provider must double the PSA result to accurately interpret it. Failure to account for this could miss prostate cancer.

questions.text: A client asks about the Shingrix vaccine. The nurse correctly explains that this vaccine is recommended for:

options.A: All children under age 12
options.B: Adults aged 50 years and older
options.C: Immunocompromised clients only
options.D: Anyone who has never had chickenpox

questions.rationale: Shingrix vaccine is recommended for adults age 50 and older. The professor specified age >60 specifically, but the standard recommendation is 50+.

questions.text: When caring for a client with cholecystitis, the nurse understands that which imaging test is the first-line choice?

options.A: CT scan
options.B: MRI of the abdomen
options.C: Ultrasound
options.D: Barium swallow

questions.rationale: Ultrasound is the first-choice imaging modality for cholecystitis. HIDA scan is used when more functional information is needed.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the diet of a client with Crohn's disease who has a fistula. Which dietary modification is most appropriate?

options.A: Low calorie, low protein diet
options.B: High calorie, high protein diet
options.C: Clear liquid diet permanently
options.D: Renal diet to protect kidney function

questions.rationale: High calorie, high protein diet is needed for fistula healing in Crohn's disease. Fistulas create significant energy and protein losses.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about peptic ulcer disease (PUD) management is correct?

options.A: Triple therapy includes two PPIs and one antibiotic
options.B: Bismuth helps by neutralizing stomach acid
options.C: Triple therapy includes a PPI plus two antibiotics, with or without bismuth
options.D: NSAIDs should be continued unless severe pain develops

questions.rationale: PUD triple therapy = PPI + two antibiotics (usually amoxicillin + another) ± bismuth. Bismuth inhibits H. pylori binding, not acid neutralization.

questions.text: A client with gastroenteritis asks if they should take loperamide (Imodium) to stop the diarrhea. The nurse's best response is:

options.A: "Yes, always take loperamide to prevent dehydration."
options.B: "Avoid loperamide if your diarrhea is infectious or accompanied by fever or bloody stools."
options.C: "Loperamide is the first-line treatment for all types of gastroenteritis."
options.D: "Take loperamide only if your symptoms last more than a week."

questions.rationale: Loperamide is contraindicated if diarrhea is infectious (e.g., bacterial), accompanied by fever, or is bloody. Using it in these cases traps the pathogens in the colon.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a client being discharged after a TURP. Which instruction about fluid intake is most important?

options.A: Limit fluid intake to 1,000 mL per day to prevent swelling
options.B: Drink up to 2,500 mL per day unless contraindicated to help with dysuria
options.C: Drink only distilled water for the first week
options.D: Restrict fluids at night to prevent nocturia

questions.rationale: Clients discharged with a catheter post-TURP should drink up to 2,500 mL/day (unless contraindicated) to dilute urine, prevent clots, and reduce dysuria.

questions.text: A client with a bowel obstruction is placed on NPO status and has an NG tube inserted. Which additional intervention does the nurse anticipate?

options.A: Preparing for an immediate colonoscopy
options.B: Initiating Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN)
options.C: Administering high-dose oral laxatives
options.D: Encouraging the client to walk to stimulate bowel function

questions.rationale: TPN is initiated when a client with bowel obstruction is NPO and cannot receive enteral nutrition, to maintain nutritional status.

questions.text: Which sign involves the patient's knees involuntarily flexing when the nurse flexes the client's neck, indicating meningeal irritation?

options.A: Kernig's sign
options.B: Babinski sign
options.C: Brudzinski's sign
options.D: Chvostek's sign

questions.rationale: Brudzinski's sign: when the nurse flexes the client's neck, the knees involuntarily flex. Kernig's sign is inability to extend the knee when hip is flexed.

questions.text: A client is diagnosed with gastric (stomach) ulcer. The nurse understands that the pain pattern associated with this ulcer type is:

options.A: Pain that significantly improves with eating
options.B: Pain that may worsen with eating
options.C: Pain exclusively at night
options.D: Pain only in the right lower quadrant

questions.rationale: Gastric ulcer pain may worsen with eating (food stimulates acid production in a stomach with a compromised mucosa). Duodenal ulcer pain often improves with eating.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing TPN orders. Which action demonstrates correct TPN administration technique?

options.A: Infusing TPN through a peripheral IV in the hand
options.B: Changing the TPN IV tubing every 72 hours
options.C: Monitoring blood glucose levels regularly
options.D: Stopping TPN abruptly when the bag is empty and no replacement is available

questions.rationale: Blood glucose monitoring is an essential nursing intervention with TPN because TPN contains dextrose and can cause hyperglycemia.

questions.text: Which of the following findings is the classic assessment sign of peritonitis?

options.A: Rebound tenderness with soft abdomen
options.B: Rigid, board-like abdomen
options.C: Hyperactive bowel sounds
options.D: Mild epigastric burning

questions.rationale: Rigid, board-like abdomen is the CLASSIC sign of peritonitis (involuntary muscular contraction protecting the inflamed peritoneum).

questions.text: A client reports that their shingles pain began 3 days before any visible rash appeared. The nurse recognizes this as:

options.A: A sign of secondary bacterial infection
options.B: The normal prodromal phase of herpes zoster
options.C: Evidence that the diagnosis is incorrect
options.D: An allergic reaction to medication

questions.rationale: The prodromal phase of shingles involves burning pain along the dermatome BEFORE the rash appears. This is a classic clinical pattern.

questions.text: Which type of BPH medication works by blocking alpha-1 receptors to relax smooth muscle and open the urethra?

options.A: Finasteride
options.B: Mesalamine
options.C: Prazosin
options.D: Famotidine

questions.rationale: Prazosin is an alpha-1 blocker that relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, opening the urethra. Finasteride shrinks the prostate itself.

questions.text: The nurse is providing discharge teaching to a client with GERD. Which position modification should be recommended?

options.A: Sleep flat on the back
options.B: Elevate the head of the bed using blocks or a wedge pillow
options.C: Sleep on the right side to prevent reflux
options.D: Lie on the left side with knees bent after eating

questions.rationale: Elevating the head of the bed (via blocks under bed frame or wedge pillow) uses gravity to prevent acid reflux during sleep.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease is hospitalized for a severe exacerbation. The nurse anticipates which nutritional intervention?

options.A: Starting a high-fiber oral diet
options.B: Beginning TPN for complete bowel rest
options.C: Offering a high-residue diet to stimulate the bowel
options.D: Prescribing a high-fat diet to replace lost energy

questions.rationale: For severe Crohn's exacerbations requiring hospitalization, bowel rest is achieved with TPN (IV nutrition bypasses the GI tract entirely).

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with cholecystitis who is to receive morphine for pain. The nurse educates the client that morphine may cause which specific gallbladder-related side effect?

options.A: Increased bile production
options.B: Biliary spasm at the sphincter of Oddi
options.C: Dissolution of gallstones
options.D: Relaxation of the bile duct

questions.rationale: Morphine and hydromorphone can cause biliary spasm at the sphincter of Oddi, potentially worsening biliary colic. However, they are still used because pain control is the priority.

questions.text: The nurse suspects a client has a perforated peptic ulcer. Which assessment finding supports this suspicion?

options.A: Pain that is relieved after eating
options.B: Sudden onset of severe, diffuse abdominal pain with a rigid abdomen
options.C: Dark tarry stools with gradual onset
options.D: Projectile vomiting of undigested food

questions.rationale: Sudden onset of severe, diffuse abdominal pain with rigidity (board-like abdomen) signals perforation and impending peritonitis — a surgical emergency.

questions.text: A client newly diagnosed with BPH asks what causes the urinary problems. The nurse explains that:

options.A: The kidney is producing less urine due to age
options.B: The prostate gland enlarges and compresses the urethra, obstructing urine flow
options.C: The bladder wall has become too thick to contract
options.D: Scarring from previous infections blocks the urinary tract

questions.rationale: BPH = benign prostatic hyperplasia = the prostate enlarges, compresses the urethra, and obstructs urine flow causing all urinary symptoms.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client receiving TPN. Which complication is specific to the route of TPN administration?

options.A: Hypoglycemia from excess insulin
options.B: Central line-associated bloodstream infection
options.C: Bowel perforation
options.D: Diarrhea from formula intolerance

questions.rationale: Central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) is the specific complication of TPN that is related to the central line administration route.

questions.text: Which of the following is a characteristic finding specific to Crohn's disease that distinguishes it from ulcerative colitis?

options.A: Begins in the rectum
options.B: Only affects the mucosa
options.C: Involves skip lesions and cobblestoning
options.D: Causes tenesmus

questions.rationale: Skip lesions and cobblestoning are hallmark characteristics of Crohn's disease. UC is continuous (no skipping) and starts in the rectum.

questions.text: A client is receiving ceftriaxone for bacterial meningitis. The nurse reviews the allergy list and notes a documented penicillin allergy. What action should the nurse take?

options.A: Administer the medication since ceftriaxone is not a penicillin
options.B: Hold the medication and notify the provider of the cross-sensitivity risk between cephalosporins and penicillin
options.C: Administer a reduced dose to test for sensitivity
options.D: Administer diphenhydramine first and then proceed with the medication

questions.rationale: Ceftriaxone is a cephalosporin, and there is cross-reactivity with penicillin allergies. The nurse must notify the provider before administering.

questions.text: A client with appendicitis is being prepared for surgery. Which action should the nurse avoid?

options.A: Administering IV fluids
options.B: Keeping the client NPO
options.C: Applying a heating pad to the right lower abdomen
options.D: Assessing pain level

questions.rationale: Heat applied to the abdomen in appendicitis increases blood circulation, worsens inflammation, and significantly increases the risk of perforation. This is an absolute contraindication.

questions.text: For a client with bowel obstruction, the nurse notes that ordered analgesics have not been given. The rationale for withholding pain medication during the initial workup is:

options.A: The client's pain is not severe enough to warrant analgesics
options.B: Analgesics may mask signs of peritonitis or perforation
options.C: Analgesics will worsen bowel motility
options.D: The medications ordered are contraindicated with the NG tube

questions.rationale: Analgesics are often held during the initial workup of bowel obstruction to avoid masking signs of perforation or peritonitis that would change the clinical picture.

questions.text: The nurse is educating a group of incoming college freshmen about meningitis prevention. Which recommendation should the nurse include?

options.A: Take daily prophylactic antibiotics during the school year
options.B: Receive the meningococcal vaccine if not already immunized (recommended at age 12, booster at 16)
options.C: Avoid all shared living spaces
options.D: Wear a mask at all times in dormitories

questions.rationale: The meningococcal vaccine is recommended at age 12 with a booster at 16. This is especially important for people entering crowded living situations like college dormitories.

questions.text: A client is on a renal diet. Which dietary restrictions does this diet include? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Restricted potassium
options.B: Restricted sodium
options.C: Restricted phosphorus
options.D: Restricted carbohydrates
options.E: Restricted fat

questions.rationale: A renal diet restricts potassium, sodium, and phosphorus — all substances the kidneys would normally excrete. It may also restrict protein.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with gastroenteritis. Which finding warrants consideration of hospitalization?

options.A: Mild nausea without vomiting
options.B: Loose stools for 1 day
options.C: An elderly client with signs of dehydration and oliguria
options.D: A 25-year-old with low-grade fever

questions.rationale: Elderly clients with signs of dehydration (oliguria, poor skin turgor, hypotension) are at higher risk for complications and often require hospitalization for IV fluid management.

questions.text: Which of the following most accurately describes the mechanism of action of pantoprazole (Protonix)?

options.A: Blocks H2 receptors to reduce gastric acid
options.B: Coats the ulcer surface to form a protective barrier
options.C: Blocks the H⁺/K⁺ ATPase proton pump, reducing gastric acid secretion
options.D: Neutralizes gastric acid by buffering

questions.rationale: Pantoprazole blocks the H⁺/K⁺ ATPase (proton pump) enzyme, directly preventing acid secretion. Famotidine blocks H2 receptors (different mechanism).

questions.text: Following a laparoscopic appendectomy, the nurse provides discharge teaching. Which statement by the client indicates they need further teaching?

options.A: "I can return to work in 1–2 weeks."
options.B: "I should report fever, increasing pain, or redness at the incision site."
options.C: "I will be discharged the same day of surgery."
options.D: "I should avoid getting out of bed for the first 48 hours after surgery."

questions.rationale: After laparoscopic appendectomy, clients should be gotten out of bed that same evening to prevent atelectasis — avoiding ambulation for 48 hours is incorrect and potentially harmful.

questions.text: Which statement accurately describes sucralfate?

options.A: It is a PPI that reduces gastric acid
options.B: It is a mucosal protectant that must be given on an empty stomach and separated from other medications by 2 hours
options.C: It is an H2 blocker that can cause confusion in elderly clients
options.D: It is an antacid that neutralizes stomach acid quickly

questions.rationale: Sucralfate is a mucosal protectant that forms a protective barrier over ulcers. It must be given on an empty stomach, and other medications must be separated by 2 hours (sucralfate impairs absorption).

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis undergoes surgery and has an ileostomy created. The nurse teaches the client what to expect regarding initial output. What is the correct description?

options.A: Formed, brown stool from day one
options.B: Loose, dark green, bloody output initially, becoming thicker and yellow/brown over time
options.C: Clear, watery output for the first month
options.D: No output for 72 hours, then normal formed stool

questions.rationale: Post-ileostomy, initial output is loose, dark green, and bloody, then becomes thicker and turns yellow/brown as the remaining bowel adapts.

questions.text: A client with BPH is taking prazosin and calls the clinic to report feeling dizzy when getting out of bed. The nurse recognizes this as:

options.A: A sign of allergic reaction requiring immediate epinephrine
options.B: Normal response to aging in elderly men
options.C: Orthostatic hypotension, a known side effect of prazosin
options.D: Evidence of prostate enlargement worsening

questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is the primary side effect of prazosin (alpha-1 blocker). The blood vessels dilate, reducing BP when standing — hence the dizziness upon rising.

questions.text: Which lab finding would be most consistent with strangulated bowel obstruction?

options.A: Normal WBC with normal H&H
options.B: WBC elevated only if there is a fever
options.C: Elevated WBC indicating possible strangulation
options.D: Low WBC consistent with immunosuppression

questions.rationale: WBC is normally normal or only mildly elevated in simple bowel obstruction, but becomes significantly elevated when strangulation (loss of blood supply) occurs.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with shingles near the eye area. Which complication should be specifically monitored?

options.A: Acute hearing loss
options.B: Bell's palsy
options.C: Eye infection and potential vision damage
options.D: Meningitis

questions.rationale: When shingles affects the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve (herpes zoster ophthalmicus), eye infection and potential vision damage are serious complications to monitor.

questions.text: The nurse instructs a client with peptic ulcer disease to avoid which of the following? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: NSAIDs
options.B: Alcohol
options.C: Tobacco smoking
options.D: Antacids
options.E: Spicy foods

questions.rationale: NSAIDs destroy the protective mucus layer, alcohol irritates the mucosa, tobacco impairs healing and increases acid, and spicy foods irritate existing ulcers. Antacids are often USED to treat PUD (not avoided).

questions.text: A client with meningitis develops a sudden decrease in level of consciousness and changes in pupil reactivity. The nurse recognizes these as signs of which complication?

options.A: Onset of meningococcal rash
options.B: Increased intracranial pressure
options.C: Adverse effect of antibiotics
options.D: Normal response to lumbar puncture

questions.rationale: Decreased level of consciousness and pupil changes in meningitis indicate increased

questions.text: Which of the following dietary items is NOT allowed on a clear liquid diet?

options.A: Apple juice
options.B: Gelatin
options.C: Clear broth
options.D: Tomato soup with cream

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with cholecystitis underwent an open cholecystectomy and has a T-tube in place. The nurse understands that the T-tube:

options.A: Provides continuous IV fluid administration
options.B: Drains bile from the bile duct while healing occurs
options.C: Decompresses the stomach post-operatively

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with peritonitis. Which set of assessment findings does the nurse expect? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Rigid, board-like abdomen
options.B: Hyperactive bowel sounds
options.C: Tachycardia and hypotension
options.D: Decreased urine output
options.E: Hiccups

questions.rationale: Peritonitis presents with rigid/board-like abdomen, tachycardia, hypotension (from third-spacing), decreased urine output (poor renal perfusion), and hiccups (diaphragmatic irritation). Bowel sounds are diminishing or absent — NOT hyperactive.

questions.text: A client taking famotidine (Pepcid) for GERD is an 80-year-old. Which adverse effect should the nurse be most vigilant about monitoring in this population?

options.A: Constipation and dry mouth
options.B: Confusion and mental status changes
options.C: Hypomagnesemia and muscle cramps
options.D: C. difficile-associated diarrhea

questions.rationale: Famotidine (H2 blocker) causes confusion and mental status changes specifically in elderly clients. This is a well-known adverse effect that requires vigilant monitoring in clients over 65.

questions.text: The nurse is performing CBI output calculation after TURP. The irrigation bag started at 3,000 mL. At the end of shift, 700 mL remains. The total drainage bag reads 2,900 mL. What is the client's true urine output?

options.B: 600 mL
options.D: 700 mL

questions.rationale: True urine = Total drainage − Irrigation infused. Irrigation infused = 3,000 − 700 = 2,300 mL. True urine = 2,900 − 2,300 = 600 mL. Always subtract what went in to find what the kidneys produced.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease reports steatorrhea. The nurse interprets this as a sign of:

options.A: Significant upper GI bleeding
options.B: Impaired fat absorption (malabsorption)
options.C: Adequate dietary fat intake
options.D: Hepatic dysfunction

questions.rationale: Steatorrhea (fatty, pale, foul-smelling stools) in Crohn's disease results from impaired fat absorption (malabsorption) due to disease of the terminal ileum, where bile salts are reabsorbed.

questions.text: The nurse is admitting a client with suspected meningitis. Which action should be taken first before initiating IV antibiotics?

options.A: Perform a lumbar puncture immediately
options.B: Draw blood cultures (C&S) before starting antibiotics
options.C: Administer corticosteroids to reduce inflammation
options.D: Obtain a CT scan and wait for results before any treatment

questions.rationale: Blood cultures (C&S) must be drawn BEFORE starting antibiotics so that the causative organism can be identified. Antibiotics should not be delayed to wait for LP results — draw cultures, then start antibiotics.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with a bowel obstruction. Which assessment finding indicates the obstruction may be complicated by strangulation?

options.A: Intermittent colicky abdominal pain
options.B: Absence of bowel sounds
options.C: Constant, severe abdominal pain with fever and elevated WBC
options.D: Nausea and vomiting without abdominal tenderness

questions.rationale: Constant, severe unrelenting pain (versus colicky intermittent pain), fever, and elevated WBC are hallmarks of strangulation — meaning the bowel has lost its blood supply and is dying. This is a surgical emergency.

questions.text: A nurse is reviewing the medication administration record for a client receiving sucralfate. The nurse notes that a morning dose of levothyroxine is also scheduled for the same time. What is the correct action?

options.A: Mix the sucralfate and levothyroxine together in 30 mL of water
options.B: Administer both medications simultaneously since they target different organs
options.C: Separate the sucralfate from the levothyroxine by at least 2 hours
options.D: Hold the sucralfate and notify the provider

questions.rationale: Sucralfate impairs absorption of many medications. Other drugs must be given at least 2 hours before or after sucralfate to ensure proper drug absorption. Giving them simultaneously renders other drugs less effective.

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis is describing their bowel elimination pattern. Which description is most consistent with UC?

options.A: 5–6 soft, loose stools per day with steatorrhea
options.B: 10–20 liquid, bloody stools per day with tenesmus
options.C: 1–2 formed stools per day with mild cramping
options.D: Obstipation (no stool passage) alternating with diarrhea

questions.rationale: UC is characterized by 10–20 liquid, bloody stools per day and tenesmus (constant urge to defecate even when bowel is empty). The 5–6 soft stools with steatorrhea description fits Crohn's disease.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing lab results for a client receiving TPN. Which finding requires immediate intervention?

options.A: Blood glucose of 120 mg/dL
options.B: Serum sodium of 138 mEq/L
options.C: Blood glucose of 300 mg/dL
options.D: Serum potassium of 4.0 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Blood glucose of 300 mg/dL represents significant hyperglycemia and requires immediate intervention (insulin administration). TPN's high dextrose content makes hyperglycemia the most important metabolic complication to monitor.

questions.text: A client diagnosed with BPH is prescribed finasteride. The client's wife, who is pregnant, asks if she can help cut the pill in half since the dose seems large. The nurse's best response is:

options.A: "That's fine, as long as she washes her hands immediately after."
options.B: "She should not handle crushed or cut finasteride tablets — it can harm the developing male fetus through skin absorption."
options.C: "The pill can be cut if she wears gloves."
options.D: "This is only a concern if she has direct skin-to-skin contact with the patient."

questions.rationale: Finasteride can be absorbed through the skin. If a pregnant woman handles crushed/broken tablets, the drug crosses the placenta and blocks DHT production in a male fetus, causing abnormal genital development. This is absolutely contraindicated.

questions.text: A client is being treated for peptic ulcer disease with PPI triple therapy. Which antibiotic in this regimen should prompt the nurse to ask about penicillin allergies?

options.A: Azithromycin
options.B: Ciprofloxacin
options.C: Amoxicillin
options.D: Metronidazole

questions.rationale: PPI triple therapy typically includes amoxicillin, which is a penicillin-class antibiotic. Clients with documented penicillin allergies must be assessed before this drug is administered.

questions.text: The nurse is monitoring a client with a nasogastric tube connected to low intermittent suction for bowel obstruction. Which assessment finding should be reported to the provider immediately?

options.A: Output of 200 mL of bilious (yellow-green) fluid over 4 hours
options.B: Output of bright red blood from the NG tube
options.C: The client reports slight nasal discomfort from the tube
options.D: The NG tube drainage has decreased from 300 to 100 mL over the past hour

questions.rationale: Bright red blood from the NG tube indicates active upper GI bleeding — this is an emergency requiring immediate provider notification. Bilious output is expected with bowel obstruction decompression.

questions.text: A client with shingles is concerned because they noticed the rash is involving their forehead and the tip of their nose. The nurse recognizes this pattern as indicating which complication risk?

options.A: Bell's palsy
options.B: Postherpetic neuralgia
options.C: Eye infection (herpes zoster ophthalmicus)
options.D: Bacterial superinfection of the rash

questions.rationale: Involvement of the forehead and tip of the nose indicates the nasociliary branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve is affected — this is herpes zoster ophthalmicus, placing the eye at serious risk.

questions.text: The nurse is implementing droplet precautions for a client with bacterial meningitis. Which actions are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Place the client in a negative-pressure airborne isolation room
options.B: Wear a surgical mask when within 3 feet of the client
options.C: Maintain precautions until the client has received 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy
options.D: Educate close contacts about prophylactic antibiotics
options.E: Use standard precautions since it is a neurological — not respiratory — condition

questions.rationale: Droplet precautions require a surgical mask within 3 feet, maintained until 24 hours of effective antibiotics, and close contacts should receive prophylactic antibiotics. A negativepressure room is for airborne (not droplet) precautions.

questions.text: A post-operative client following open cholecystectomy has a T-tube in place. The nurse notes that the T-tube drainage has changed from golden yellow-brown to dark green. What is the most appropriate action?

options.A: This is expected — document and continue monitoring
options.B: Clamp the T-tube immediately
options.C: Notify the provider — this color change may indicate complications
options.D: Irrigate the T-tube with normal saline

questions.rationale: T-tube drainage should be golden yellow-brown (normal bile). Dark green drainage may indicate infection, biliary obstruction, or other complications — the provider must be notified promptly.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with gastroenteritis. The nurse prepares to administer loperamide as ordered. Which assessment finding should cause the nurse to hold the medication and contact the provider?

options.A: The client reports abdominal cramping
options.B: The client has a temperature of 101.8°F and bloody stools
options.C: The client has had 6 loose stools in 24 hours
options.D: The client is over 65 years of age

questions.rationale: Loperamide is absolutely contraindicated when diarrhea is accompanied by fever and bloody stools, as these signs suggest infectious etiology. Slowing motility traps pathogens and toxins in the colon, worsening the condition.

questions.text: A client with GERD has a history of taking ibuprofen daily for arthritis. The nurse teaches the client that NSAIDs worsen GERD because they:

options.A: Increase esophageal motility
options.B: Block prostaglandins needed to maintain the protective mucous layer and relax the LES
options.C: Increase gastric acid secretion by stimulating the proton pump
options.D: Cause direct bile reflux into the esophagus

questions.rationale: NSAIDs block prostaglandins, which are required both to produce the protective mucus layer AND to maintain LES tone. Loss of prostaglandins weakens both defenses simultaneously, contributing to GERD and ulcer formation.

questions.text: Which of the following is a mechanical cause of bowel obstruction? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Adhesions from prior surgery
options.B: Hypokalemia causing ileus
options.C: Hernia trapping a segment of bowel
options.D: Post-operative ileus
options.E: Intussusception

questions.rationale: Mechanical causes physically block the bowel: adhesions (scar tissue), hernias (trapping bowel), and intussusception (telescoping). Hypokalemia and post-op handling cause nonmechanical (paralytic ileus) obstruction.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with cholecystitis who is scheduled for a HIDA scan. Which pre-procedure instruction is essential?

options.A: Increase fluid intake to 3,000 mL the morning of the scan
options.B: The client must be NPO before the scan
options.C: Administer a high-fat meal to stimulate the gallbladder before imaging
options.D: No special preparation is needed

questions.rationale: The HIDA (hepatobiliary) scan requires the client to be NPO. The scan visualizes bile flow through the biliary system — food intake would trigger bile release and interfere with the test.

questions.text: Following a right hemicolectomy for a perforated appendix with peritonitis, the nurse maintains strict sterile technique during wound care. The rationale is:

options.A: To prevent the client from feeling the pain of wound care
options.B: To satisfy Joint Commission documentation requirements
options.C: The peritoneal space is sterile — introduction of any pathogens increases the risk of further infection
options.D: Wound drains are sterile devices that require sterile technique by default

questions.rationale: The peritoneal cavity is a sterile space. During peritonitis surgery (exploratory laparotomy and peritoneal irrigation), strict sterile technique must be maintained throughout to prevent introducing additional pathogens into the already-infected sterile space.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease is being discharged. The nurse teaches which skin care measure specifically related to fistulas?

options.A: Keep the fistula tightly covered with an occlusive dressing at all times
options.B: Apply meticulous skin care around the fistula opening to protect surrounding tissue from enzymatic drainage
options.C: Pack the fistula opening with sterile gauze and leave undisturbed for 48 hours
options.D: Apply a heating pad over the fistula to promote drainage

questions.rationale: Fistula drainage contains intestinal enzymes and bile salts that cause severe enzymatic skin breakdown. Meticulous skin care around the fistula opening is essential to protect surrounding tissue from these caustic secretions.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client's chart before administering ciprofloxacin for meningitis. Which co-scheduled medication on the MAR requires the nurse to adjust timing?

options.A: Metoprolol (beta-blocker)
options.B: Calcium carbonate antacid
options.C: Furosemide (loop diuretic)
options.D: Ondansetron (antiemetic)

questions.rationale: Ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) must not be given with calcium, iron, or antacids because these minerals chelate (bind) the drug and significantly reduce its absorption. Calcium carbonate antacid is the specific concern here.

questions.text: A client with peptic ulcer disease underwent an EGD with endoscopic hemostasis 2 hours ago. The nurse receives the client from the recovery room. What is the priority assessment?

options.A: Assess the client's gag reflex before offering ice chips or fluids
options.B: Assess pain level using 0–10 scale
options.C: Obtain orthostatic blood pressures
options.D: Review the discharge instructions

questions.rationale: This mirrors the actual NCLEX Challenge 47.2 from the provided images. After endoscopy with moderate sedation and an NPO period, the priority is to assess the gag reflex before offering any ice chips or fluids to prevent aspiration.

questions.text: The nurse is reinforcing teaching for a client being discharged with a new ileostomy following surgery for ulcerative colitis. Which instruction is most critical for skin protection?

options.A: "Change your pouch only when it becomes completely full."
options.B: "The ileostomy pouch must be worn at all times — intestinal enzymes and bile salts in the output will severely irritate your skin."
options.C: "Rinse the stoma with hydrogen peroxide daily to prevent infection."
options.D: "You can leave the stoma uncovered for short periods of air drying."

questions.rationale: The ileostomy pouch must be worn at all times. Intestinal enzymes and bile salts in the output are highly corrosive — even brief skin exposure causes painful chemical burns. This is the most critical skin protection measure.

questions.text: A client is recovering from appendicitis surgery (laparoscopic). The nurse is assessing at 8 hours post-op. The client has not yet ambulated. Which action should the nurse take?

options.A: Allow the client to continue resting — ambulation begins day 2
options.B: Assist the client out of bed — ambulation should begin the same evening as surgery
options.C: Consult physical therapy before attempting any ambulation
options.D: Administer pain medication and reassess in 4 hours before attempting ambulation

questions.rationale: After laparoscopic appendectomy, ambulation should begin the same evening as surgery. Early ambulation prevents atelectasis, pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis, and promotes GI motility recovery.

questions.text: A client with meningitis has a serum WBC of 28,000/mm³ and blood cultures are pending. The provider orders broad-spectrum antibiotics. The nurse understands that antibiotics should be started:

options.A: Only after the lumbar puncture results are back
options.B: Only after the blood culture results are back and sensitivities are identified
options.C: Immediately — after blood cultures are drawn but without waiting for LP or culture results
options.D: Only if the client's temperature rises above 103°F

questions.rationale: Draw blood cultures first, then start antibiotics immediately — do NOT wait for culture results, LP results, or temperature thresholds. In bacterial meningitis, every hour of delay in antibiotic treatment increases mortality risk.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a client with GERD who is 35 weeks pregnant. Which advice is most appropriate for this client?

options.A: "Avoid H2 blockers entirely during pregnancy."
options.B: "Sleeping flat is best during pregnancy to avoid falling."
options.C: "Wear comfortable, non-restrictive clothing and eat small, frequent meals."
options.D: "Take aspirin at night for the pain."

questions.rationale: Pregnancy increases intraabdominal pressure (a major GERD trigger). Comfortable, nonrestrictive clothing and small frequent meals are safe, appropriate interventions. Aspirin is contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.text: A client hospitalized with a bowel obstruction asks why they cannot have pain medication. The nurse's best explanation is:

options.A: "Pain medication is not safe for your condition."
options.B: "The doctor does not believe your pain is severe enough."
options.C: "During the initial workup, we need to accurately assess your abdomen — pain medication can mask signs of perforation or worsening condition."
options.D: "We are waiting for your blood pressure to stabilize first."

questions.rationale: This is the accurate clinical rationale: pain medication can alter the abdominal exam during workup, potentially masking signs of perforation or peritonitis that would change the diagnosis and surgical decision-making.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the diet order for a client with an active ulcerative colitis flare. Which diet is most appropriate?

options.A: High-fiber diet to bulk up stools
options.B: Lactose-free, low-fiber, low-residue diet avoiding GI stimulants
options.C: Regular diet with adequate hydration
options.D: High-protein, high-fat diet to replace nutritional losses

questions.rationale: During UC flares, the diet should minimize bowel stimulation: lactose-free (milk triggers UC), low-fiber, low-residue, avoiding caffeine, alcohol, and smoking. High-fiber diet would worsen diarrhea.

questions.text: A 65-year-old male client comes to the clinic reporting nocturia (waking up to urinate 4 times per night), weak urine stream, and a feeling of incomplete emptying after voiding. The nurse anticipates the provider will use which tool to systematically assess symptoms?

options.A: Digital rectal exam only
options.B: International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS)
options.C: Post-void residual bladder scan
options.D: PSA level alone

questions.rationale: The International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS) is a validated 7-question tool that scores urinary symptoms systematically (incomplete emptying, frequency, intermittency, urgency, weak stream, straining, nocturia).

questions.text: A client develops postherpetic neuralgia following shingles. The nurse anticipates which type of medication may be prescribed for this neuropathic pain?

options.A: Ibuprofen
options.B: Gabapentin
options.C: Loperamide
options.D: Mesalamine

questions.rationale: Gabapentin (an anticonvulsant/neuropathic pain agent) is used to treat postherpetic neuralgia. NSAIDs and acetaminophen are insufficient for neuropathic pain. Loperamide and mesalamine are GI drugs.

questions.text: A client post-TURP has continuous bladder irrigation running. The nurse notes that the urine in the drainage bag is bright red with clots. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.A: Clamp the catheter and notify the provider
options.B: Increase the CBI irrigation rate to help flush the clots
options.C: Administer a bolus of IV fluids
options.D: Document the finding and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: When urine is bright red with clots, the CBI rate should be increased to flush the clots and prevent catheter obstruction. Clamping would cause dangerous bladder distension. Document and notify provider if the bleeding is excessive.

questions.text: The nurse is assessing a client for Kernig's sign. Which technique correctly describes this assessment?

options.A: Flex the client's neck and observe if the knees flex involuntarily
options.B: Flex the client's hip to 90° while supine, then attempt to extend the knee — a positive sign is pain or resistance
options.C: Press firmly over the McBurney's point and release quickly to check for rebound tenderness
options.D: Tap the facial nerve in front of the ear and observe for facial muscle twitching

questions.rationale: Kernig's sign: with the client supine, flex the hip to 90°, then attempt to extend the knee. Positive if there is pain or resistance — indicates meningeal irritation. Brudzinski's is the neck-flex-causes-knee-flex test.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with gastroenteritis caused by Norovirus. Which statement about Norovirus is accurate?

options.A: Norovirus is a bacterial infection requiring antibiotics
options.B: Norovirus is the leading cause of foodborne illness with peak seasons November through April
options.C: Norovirus primarily affects immunocompromised clients only
options.D: Norovirus is treated with antiviral medications within 72 hours

questions.rationale: Norovirus is the leading cause of foodborne illness and peaks November through April (not summer). It is viral (antibiotics don't help), self-limiting, and affects all populations (not just immunocompromised).

questions.text: A client with peptic ulcer disease has a positive H. pylori test. The nurse teaches the client about triple therapy. Which statement by the client shows correct understanding?

options.A: "I only need to take the medication until my pain goes away."
options.B: "I should take the antibiotic on an empty stomach for best effect."
options.C: "I need to complete the full course of all medications to eradicate the bacteria, even after I feel better."
options.D: "One antibiotic combined with an antacid should be enough."

questions.rationale: H. pylori eradication requires completing the full antibiotic course even after symptoms resolve. Stopping early leads to antibiotic resistance and treatment failure. This is a critical patient education point.

questions.text: The nurse is assessing a client with suspected appendicitis who reports the pain has suddenly disappeared. The nurse's priority response is:

options.A: Reassure the client that the appendicitis has resolved
options.B: Document the finding and reduce pain monitoring frequency
options.C: Recognize this as a potential sign of perforation — assess for rigidity, fever, and signs of peritonitis immediately
options.D: Prepare to discharge the client as the condition appears to have improved

questions.rationale: Sudden relief of appendicitis pain is a dangerous sign — it may indicate perforation. When the appendix ruptures, the pressure is briefly relieved, but the situation rapidly becomes life-threatening as peritonitis develops.

questions.text: A client admitted with cholecystitis has the following labs: elevated WBC, elevated lipase, and elevated bilirubin. The nurse recognizes that the elevated lipase indicates:

options.A: The gallstones have dissolved
options.B: Bile reflux has activated the pancreas — pancreatitis may be developing
options.C: The liver is failing
options.D: The cystic duct has been successfully opened

questions.rationale: Elevated lipase indicates pancreatic enzyme activation. Bile from an obstructed cystic duct backing up can reflux into the pancreatic duct, prematurely activating pancreatic enzymes and causing pancreatitis.

questions.text: Which of the following assessment findings in a client with a bowel obstruction would suggest the obstruction is in the large intestine rather than the small intestine?

options.A: Projectile vomiting early in the course
options.B: Rapid onset of symptoms with severe abdominal distension
options.C: Gradual onset with prominent abdominal distension and obstipation
options.D: Immediate high-volume emesis of bilious material

questions.rationale: Large bowel obstruction has a more gradual onset (large bowel has more capacity to accommodate before symptoms appear), prominent abdominal distension, and obstipation. Small bowel obstruction causes earlier, more acute vomiting.

questions.text: The nurse is providing health promotion teaching to a 58-year-old client who has never had shingles but had chickenpox as a child. Which statement by the nurse is correct?

options.A: "Since you never had shingles, you are not at risk."
options.B: "The virus that caused your chickenpox is still in your body — you should discuss getting the Shingrix vaccine with your provider."
options.C: "You should get the varicella vaccine immediately."
options.D: "Shingles only occurs in people who are immunocompromised — you are at low risk."

questions.rationale: Anyone who had chickenpox carries dormant VZV in their nerve ganglia for life. The Shingrix vaccine (for adults 50+) dramatically reduces the risk of reactivation. This client at age 58 is a candidate.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with peritonitis who has a decreased urine output of 20 mL/hour. The nurse interprets this as:

options.A: Expected response to antibiotics
options.B: Third-spacing of fluid causing intravascular hypovolemia and poor kidney perfusion
options.C: Renal failure from antibiotic toxicity
options.D: The client is simply not drinking enough oral fluids

questions.rationale: In peritonitis, massive third-spacing (fluid shifting from the bloodstream into the inflamed peritoneal space) causes intravascular hypovolemia, reducing blood flow to the kidneys, resulting in oliguria.

questions.text: A client with GERD undergoes a Laparoscopic Nissen Fundoplication (LNF). The nurse explains the procedure as:

options.A: Removal of the lower esophageal sphincter and replacement with a prosthetic valve
options.B: Wrapping the top of the stomach around the lower esophagus to mechanically strengthen the LES
options.C: Removing the portion of the esophagus damaged by Barrett's epithelium
options.D: Placing a gastric band around the lower esophagus

questions.rationale: Laparoscopic Nissen Fundoplication wraps the fundus of the stomach around the lower esophagus, mechanically reinforcing the LES and preventing acid reflux. It is indicated when GERD causes respiratory symptoms.

questions.text: The nurse is preparing a client for a HIDA scan. In addition to NPO status, the nurse knows the provider will likely administer which medication during the procedure?

options.A: IV glucagon to slow bowel motility for better visualization
options.B: IV mannitol to increase gallbladder output
options.C: IV contrast dye to highlight the gallbladder
options.D: Oral bismuth to coat the gallbladder lining

questions.rationale: IV glucagon is administered during an MRE (and sometimes imaging procedures) to slow bowel motility, allowing better visualization of bowel structures.

questions.text: A client receiving TPN develops a fever of 102°F and chills. The central line insertion site appears red with purulent drainage. The nurse's priority action is:

options.A: Increase the TPN rate to improve nutrition and boost immune function
options.B: Administer acetaminophen for the fever and continue TPN
options.C: Notify the provider — these signs indicate central line infection requiring intervention
options.D: Flush the central line with heparin and document the findings

questions.rationale: Fever, chills, and purulent drainage at the central line site in a TPN client indicate central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) — a life-threatening complication requiring immediate provider notification and likely line removal.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a client with Crohn's disease about dietary management. Which statement by the nurse is correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: "Caffeine and alcohol can trigger exacerbations."
options.B: "Milk and gluten may cause discomfort in some patients."
options.C: "A high-calorie, high-protein diet supports healing, especially if you have fistulas."
options.D: "You should eat high-fiber foods to improve bowel function."
options.E: "Stress management is an important part of preventing flares."

questions.rationale: Caffeine, alcohol, milk, and gluten can trigger Crohn's flares. High-calorie, highprotein diet supports fistula healing. Stress management prevents exacerbations. High-fiber foods WORSEN Crohn's by increasing bowel stimulation.

questions.text: The nurse performs a focused abdominal assessment for a client with suspected peritonitis. Which finding most clearly differentiates peritonitis from a simple ileus?

options.A: Absent bowel sounds
options.B: Abdominal distension
options.C: Rigid, board-like abdomen with rebound tenderness
options.D: Nausea and vomiting

questions.rationale: Rigid, board-like abdomen with rebound tenderness specifically indicates peritoneal inflammation. Absent bowel sounds and distension can occur in both ileus and peritonitis, but rigidity with rebound tenderness is the distinguishing sign.

questions.text: The nurse is instructing a student nurse about NG tube care for a client with peptic ulcer disease and active GI bleeding. Which instruction is most important?

options.A: "Check tube placement by blowing air into the tube and listening."
options.B: "Monitor NG output carefully — bright red blood or large volume of blood indicates active bleeding."
options.C: "Flush the NG tube with 60 mL of tap water every 4 hours."
options.D: "NG tube placement only needs to be confirmed once after initial insertion."

questions.rationale: Monitoring NG output for bright red blood is the most critical safety action during active GI bleeding. Auscultation of air insufflation is NOT a reliable method to confirm placement — x-ray is the gold standard.

questions.text: The nurse is triaging four clients. Which client should be seen first?

options.A: A client with GERD reporting heartburn after eating pizza
options.B: A client with BPH complaining of nocturia and weak urine stream
options.C: A client with suspected appendicitis whose abdominal pain has suddenly shifted to diffuse, severe, and is accompanied by a rigid abdomen
options.D: A client with gastroenteritis reporting loose stools for 2 days

questions.rationale: Sudden diffuse severe pain with rigidity in an appendicitis client = perforation with peritonitis — an acute abdominal emergency requiring immediate assessment and surgical intervention. This takes priority over all other clients.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing orders for a client just diagnosed with meningitis. The provider orders a lumbar puncture AND broad-spectrum IV antibiotics. In which order should these interventions occur?

options.A: Start antibiotics, then do the lumbar puncture
options.B: Do the lumbar puncture, then start antibiotics
options.C: Draw blood cultures first, then start antibiotics while waiting for LP to be scheduled
options.D: All interventions can occur simultaneously without concern for sequence

questions.rationale: Draw blood cultures first (to identify organism before antibiotics destroy it), then start antibiotics immediately without waiting for LP results. LP will still be performed but antibiotics cannot be delayed.

questions.text: A client with BPH asks why he needs to have frequent sexual intercourse. The nurse explains that:

options.A: This has no bearing on prostate health
options.B: Frequent sexual intercourse can help reduce obstruction symptoms by releasing prostatic fluid
options.C: Sexual intercourse will worsen BPH symptoms
options.D: This recommendation only applies to clients on finasteride

questions.rationale: Frequent sexual intercourse releases prostatic fluid, which can reduce congestion in the prostate and temporarily decrease obstruction symptoms. This is a non-pharmacologic BPH management strategy.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease is being assessed for psychological health. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.A: Redirect the client away from discussion of emotional concerns and focus on physical symptoms
options.B: Acknowledge that depression and anxiety are common in Crohn's disease and provide appropriate support and referrals
options.C: Assure the client that there is no connection between stress and Crohn's disease
options.D: Schedule the client for routine psychiatric evaluation regardless of symptom presentation

questions.rationale: Depression and anxiety are documented complications of Crohn's disease. The nurse must acknowledge the psychosocial dimension, provide support, and refer to appropriate mental health resources. Stress can also trigger exacerbations.

questions.text: The nurse is evaluating the effectiveness of treatment for a client with ulcerative colitis. Which outcome indicates that treatment is working?

options.A: The client reports 15 bloody stools per day
options.B: The client's CRP and ESR are decreasing and stool frequency is reducing
options.C: The client's WBC is rising
options.D: The ileostomy is producing increasing output

questions.rationale: Treatment effectiveness in UC is measured by decreasing inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR), reduction in stool frequency, and improvement in labs (H&H improving, albumin normalizing).

questions.text: A client with peritonitis is ordered to receive hydromorphone IV. The nurse notes the client's respiratory rate is 10 breaths per minute. What is the priority action?

options.A: Administer the hydromorphone — pain management is the priority
options.B: Administer a half dose of hydromorphone
options.C: Hold the hydromorphone and notify the provider — RR < 12 is a contraindication
options.D: Administer naloxone prophylactically before giving the hydromorphone

questions.rationale: Respiratory rate < 12 breaths/minute is an absolute contraindication to opioid administration. The nurse must hold the hydromorphone and notify the provider — respiratory depression from opioids is a life-threatening risk.

questions.text: The nurse is educating a client about Curling's ulcer prevention in a burn ICU client. The nurse explains that this type of stress ulcer occurs because extensive burns:

options.A: Cause direct chemical damage to the gastric mucosa
options.B: Cause fluid losses that reduce the protective mucus layer of the GI tract
options.C: Stimulate the vagus nerve to produce excess gastric acid
options.D: Cause ischemia to the stomach from reduced blood flow

questions.rationale: Curling's ulcers occur in burn patients because extensive burns cause massive fluid and plasma losses, which reduce the protective mucous layer of the GI tract, exposing the mucosa to gastric acid.

questions.text: A client is receiving pantoprazole for GERD. Which patient education point is most important?

options.A: "Take this medication with a full glass of milk for best absorption."
options.B: "You can crush or chew the delayed-release tablets for easier swallowing."
options.C: "Take pantoprazole before meals for best effectiveness, and do not crush or chew delayed-release tablets."
options.D: "You can stop taking this medication once your heartburn improves."

questions.rationale: Pantoprazole must be taken before meals (optimally 30 minutes before) to be most effective, and delayed-release tablets must not be crushed or chewed as this destroys the enteric coating and affects drug delivery.

questions.text: A client with gastroenteritis is being treated with oral rehydration therapy. Which product is the most appropriate choice?

options.A: Water only
options.B: Orange juice
options.C: Sports drinks or Pedialyte
options.D: Milk

questions.rationale: Sports drinks (Gatorade) and Pedialyte provide electrolyte replacement in addition to fluids — the appropriate choice for oral rehydration therapy. Water alone doesn't replace electrolytes; milk and OJ are inappropriate.

questions.text: The nurse is providing post-op teaching for a client who had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Which instruction should the nurse include about the steri-strips on the incisions?

options.A: "Remove the strips after 24 hours and clean with alcohol."
options.B: "The strips will fall off on their own in approximately 10 days — do not forcibly remove them."
options.C: "Keep the strips covered with a thick occlusive dressing at all times."
options.D: "Soak the strips in water to soften them for easier removal at home."

questions.rationale: Steri-strips from laparoscopic incisions fall off on their own in approximately 10 days. Forcibly removing them can disrupt wound healing and increase infection risk.

questions.text: The nurse is obtaining a health history from a client with suspected GERD. Which medication history finding could be contributing to the client's GERD? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Daily aspirin therapy
options.B: Amlodipine (calcium channel blocker)
options.C: Isosorbide mononitrate (nitrate)
options.D: Metformin (oral hypoglycemic)
options.E: Ibuprofen (NSAID)

questions.rationale: Aspirin (NSAID), calcium channel blockers (CCBs like amlodipine), nitrates, and NSAIDs all weaken the LES or damage the gastric mucosa, contributing to GERD. Metformin does not cause GERD.

questions.text: A client is admitted with a diagnosis of viral meningitis. Which type of precautions does the nurse implement?

options.A: Droplet precautions
options.B: Airborne precautions
options.C: Standard precautions
options.D: Contact precautions

questions.rationale: Viral meningitis requires only standard precautions — viruses causing meningitis are not transmitted by respiratory droplets in the same way bacterial meningitis is. Droplet precautions are for bacterial meningitis.

questions.text: During a routine assessment, the nurse notes that a client with a long-term TPN infusion has a blood glucose of 350 mg/dL. Which intervention is the priority?

options.A: Slow the TPN infusion rate
options.B: Notify the provider and anticipate an order for insulin
options.C: Switch the TPN to D5W to decrease the glucose concentration
options.D: Discontinue the TPN immediately

questions.rationale: Blood glucose of 350 is a critical hyperglycemia value. The nurse notifies the provider and anticipates an insulin order. TPN should not be stopped abruptly (causes hypoglycemia), and the rate should not be casually adjusted without an order.

questions.text: A client with appendicitis is positioned in a fetal-like right side posture (right hip flexed). The nurse understands this position:

options.A: Is contraindicated and should be corrected
options.B: Relieves tension on the inflamed appendix, reducing pain
options.C: Is a sign of peritoneal irritation requiring surgery
options.D: Prevents the appendix from perforating

questions.rationale: The fetal-like position (right hip and knee flexed) relieves tension on the inflamed appendix by relaxing the iliopsoas muscle, reducing pain. It is therapeutic, not a sign of complications.

questions.text: The nurse is planning care for a client with cholecystitis who is NPO. Which intervention should be included in the care plan? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Administer prescribed IV ketorolac for pain
options.B: Monitor for signs of jaundice
options.C: Encourage a high-fat diet to stimulate bile release
options.D: Assess for steatorrhea (fatty stools)
options.E: Monitor liver enzymes and lipase labs

questions.rationale: NPO client with cholecystitis needs IV ketorolac for pain, monitoring for jaundice (bile duct obstruction), assessment for steatorrhea (bile not reaching intestine), and liver enzyme/lipase monitoring. High-fat diet is absolutely contraindicated — it triggers gallbladder contraction and worsens pain.

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis asks the nurse if taking ibuprofen for a headache is okay. The nurse correctly advises:

options.A: "Ibuprofen is fine in small doses."
options.B: "NSAIDs like ibuprofen can trigger UC flares — discuss pain relief alternatives with your provider."
options.C: "Take ibuprofen only with food to protect your stomach."
options.D: "NSAID use is unrelated to inflammatory bowel disease."

questions.rationale: NSAIDs (including ibuprofen) are well-documented triggers for UC flares. They should be avoided. The client should discuss acetaminophen or other alternatives with their provider.

questions.text: A client with meningitis develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Which nursing intervention is the priority?

options.A: Restrain the client to prevent injury
options.B: Insert an oral airway to prevent tongue biting
options.C: Protect the client from injury, maintain airway, and note duration and characteristics
options.D: Administer antiepileptic medication immediately without waiting for orders

questions.rationale: During a seizure, the priority nursing actions are: protect from injury (padded side rails, remove hard objects), maintain airway patency (turn on side if possible), and document duration and characteristics. Restraining causes injury; forcing an airway device causes broken teeth.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a client about GERD with Barrett's esophagus. The nurse correctly explains that Barrett's esophagus:

options.A: Is a benign finding with no risk implications
options.B: Represents precancerous tissue changes that increase the risk of esophageal cancer
options.C: Can be resolved with aggressive antacid therapy
options.D: Only occurs in clients with H. pylori
