questions.rationale: Barrett's esophagus develops when chronic acid damage causes the esophageal mucosa to be replaced by stomach-like (columnar) epithelium. This is precancerous tissue that significantly increases risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct position for the drainage bag of a client who was discharged home with an indwelling catheter post-TURP?

options.A: Above the level of the bladder
options.B: At the level of the bladder
options.C: Below the level of the bladder
options.D: The level does not matter

options.B: Hypoactive or absent bowel sounds
options.C: Normal bowel sounds 5–30 per minute
options.D: Bowel sounds heard in all four quadrants

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

options.B: "Antihistamines cause urinary retention by their anticholinergic effects, which worsen the obstruction already present."
options.C: "Antihistamines interact with prazosin and can cause a dangerous drug reaction."
options.D: "Antihistamines increase prostate size over time."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

options.B: Place the client in the Trendelenburg position
options.C: Ensure large-bore IV access is established and IV fluid resuscitation is underway
options.D: Remove the NG tube since surgery will address the obstruction

options.C: Change TPN tubing only when visibly contaminated
options.D: TPN tubing does not need routine changing

options.B: Sucralfate impairs absorption of warfarin if given simultaneously — separate by 2 hours
options.C: These drugs cannot be taken together under any circumstance
options.D: Sucralfate has no interaction with anticoagulants

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

options.C: "I can take loperamide to stop the diarrhea since I have a fever."
options.D: "I'll seek care if I don't improve within 48 hours."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with a nasogastric tube in place is to have oral medications administered. The nurse's first action is to:

options.A: Flush the tube with 60 mL of normal saline before and after all medications
options.B: Stop the tube feeding, administer medications one at a time, and flush with water between each medication
options.C: Mix all medications together and instill as a single bolus
options.D: Administer medications without stopping the feeding to maintain caloric intake

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for four clients. Using priority-setting, which client requires assessment first?

options.A: A client with UC reporting 8 bloody stools today
options.B: A client with GERD rating heartburn 5/10 after dinner
options.C: A client with meningitis whose blood pressure has dropped from 118/78 to 84/50 and is developing a petechial rash
options.D: A client with cholecystitis awaiting an elective HIDA scan

questions.rationale: Dropping BP, petechial rash in a meningitis client = meningococcal septicemia with impending septic shock. This client can die within hours without immediate intervention. This is the highest acuity situation.

questions.text: The nurse is performing a focused assessment on a client with meningitis. Which two signs, when both positive, confirm meningeal irritation? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Blumberg sign
options.B: Kernig's sign
options.C: McBurney's point tenderness
options.D: Brudzinski's sign
options.E: Chvostek's sign

questions.rationale: Kernig's sign (pain/resistance with knee extension when hip flexed) and Brudzinski's sign (involuntary knee flexion when neck is flexed) are the two classic signs of meningeal irritation. Blumberg = gallbladder/peritoneum; McBurney's = appendicitis.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease has a serum albumin of 2.1 g/dL (normal 3.5–5 g/dL), weight loss of 15 lbs in 2 months, and persistent fistula drainage. Which nutritional interventions should the nurse anticipate? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: TPN through a central line for bowel rest
options.B: High-calorie, high-protein diet or Ensure supplementation when tolerated
options.C: Renal diet to protect kidney function
options.D: Folic acid and B12 supplementation (terminal ileum malabsorption)
options.E: Monitoring H&H for anemia

questions.rationale: With a fistula, albumin of 2.1, and significant weight loss, this client has severe malnutrition. TPN for bowel rest, high-calorie/protein supplementation, B12/folate (terminal ileum malabsorption), and H&H monitoring for anemia are all appropriate. Renal diet is not indicated.

questions.text: A client is post-op day 1 following an open appendectomy for a ruptured appendix with peritonitis. Which finding would the nurse report immediately to the provider?

options.A: Urine output of 35 mL/hour
options.B: Pain rated 6/10 managed with IV hydromorphone
options.C: Temperature of 103.4°F with increasing abdominal rigidity and purulent wound drainage
options.D: Absent bowel sounds on day 1 post-op

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client on long-term pantoprazole therapy comes in for a routine visit. Which lab finding should the nurse flag as a potential adverse effect of long-term PPI use?

options.A: Elevated serum calcium
options.B: Low serum magnesium (hypomagnesemia)
options.C: Elevated B12 level
options.D: Low serum potassium

questions.rationale: Long-term PPI use causes hypomagnesemia because PPIs impair magnesium absorption in the small intestine. Hypomagnesemia causes muscle cramps, cardiac arrhythmias, and seizures in severe cases.

questions.text: The nurse receives a client from the ED with suspected bowel obstruction. The client's abdomen is distended, bowel sounds are hyperactive and high-pitched (early obstruction), and they are vomiting bilious fluid. Which interventions should the nurse initiate? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Maintain NPO status
options.B: Insert and maintain an NG tube
options.C: Administer oral laxatives to stimulate bowel movement
options.D: Establish IV access and begin fluid resuscitation
options.E: Prepare to initiate TPN if obstruction is prolonged

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with shingles on immunosuppressive therapy following an organ transplant. Which statement is most accurate regarding this client's risk?

options.A: Immunosuppressed clients have the same risk as healthy adults
options.B: Immunosuppressed clients are at higher risk for shingles reactivation, more severe disease, and complications including disseminated infection
options.C: Immunosuppression protects the client by reducing the inflammatory response
options.D: The shingles vaccine is strongly recommended for this client regardless of immune status

questions.rationale: Immunosuppressed clients (transplant recipients, HIV, cancer) are at significantly higher risk for VZV reactivation and disseminated (widespread) shingles. The live attenuated Shingrix vaccine should be discussed with the provider — live vaccines are often contraindicated in severely immunocompromised clients.

questions.text: A client with BPH has just completed a TURP. Postoperatively, the nurse calculates the following: CBI irrigation started at 2,500 mL; 300 mL remains in the bag; total drainage bag = 2,600 mL. What is the true urine output?

options.B: 400 mL
options.D: 300 mL

questions.rationale: Irrigation infused = 2,500 − 300 = 2,200 mL. True urine = 2,600 − 2,200 = 400 mL. Always: Total drainage minus irrigation infused.

questions.text: The nurse is teaching a nursing student about the difference between Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Which statement by the student demonstrates correct understanding?

options.B: "Ulcerative colitis involves transmural inflammation with skip lesions, while Crohn's is continuous."
options.C: "Crohn's disease can form fistulas because it involves all layers of the bowel wall, while UC affects only the mucosa."
options.D: "Both conditions affect only the large intestine."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

options.C: The client's temperature is 102°F
options.D: The client requests the lights be turned off due to photophobia

questions.text: A client receiving TPN through a central line suddenly develops shortness of breath, tachycardia, and severe chest pain after the nurse changed the TPN tubing. The nurse's priority action is:

options.A: Slow the TPN infusion rate and reassess in 15 minutes
options.B: Administer supplemental oxygen and elevate the head of bed
options.C: Place the client in left lateral Trendelenburg position, call for help, and notify the provider — suspect air embolism
options.D: Administer IV fluid bolus immediately and obtain ECG

questions.rationale: Air embolism after tubing changes is a life-threatening TPN complication. Positioning in left lateral Trendelenburg traps air in the right ventricle away from the pulmonary artery, buying time while calling for emergency help.

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis develops sudden onset of severe abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, and a temperature of 103°F. The abdominal x-ray shows massive colonic dilation. The nurse recognizes this as:

options.C: Toxic megacolon — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate notification and surgical intervention
options.D: Expected side effect of mesalamine therapy

questions.rationale: Toxic megacolon is characterized by massive colonic dilation visible on x-ray, absent
