questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with post-herpetic neuralgia following shingles. The client's pain is described as burning, electric shock-like, and constant at 9/10. The client is already on gabapentin 300 mg TID. Which medications might the provider add to the regimen for multimodal neuropathic pain management? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Tricyclic antidepressants (e.g., amitriptyline) — proven efficacy for neuropathic pain
options.B: Topical lidocaine patch or capsaicin cream for localized relief
options.C: Duloxetine (SNRI) — has evidence for neuropathic pain
options.D: Loperamide — reduces bowel motility to improve pain
options.E: Opioids (e.g., tramadol) may be considered for refractory cases

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with cholecystitis is found to have a gangrenous gallbladder intraoperatively. The operative note documents: "Perforated gallbladder with bile spillage into the peritoneal cavity, peritoneal lavage performed." The nurse anticipates which post-operative management priorities? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for 5–7 days post-operatively
options.B: NPO status with NG decompression until bowel function returns
options.C: High-fat diet to stimulate bile production
options.D: Monitoring for signs of peritonitis (rigidity, fever, hemodynamic instability)
options.E: TPN if ileus is prolonged

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is evaluating a client with a severe bowel obstruction. The abdominal x-ray shows a "coffee bean" sign (massive dilation of the sigmoid colon into the shape of a coffee bean). The nurse recognizes this as pathognomonic for:

options.A: Intussusception of the small bowel
options.B: Sigmoid volvulus — a large bowel obstruction caused by twisting of the sigmoid colon on its mesenteric axis, requiring emergent decompression
options.C: Paralytic ileus from post-operative handling
options.D: Colorectal carcinoma causing complete obstruction

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with meningitis and SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone) has a serum sodium of 118 mEq/L. The provider orders hypertonic saline (3% NaCl). The nurse's priority monitoring parameter while infusing 3% NaCl is:

options.A: Blood glucose, as hypertonic saline causes hyperglycemia
options.B: Rate of sodium correction — must not exceed 8–12 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis), which causes irreversible neurological damage
options.C: Urine output, targeting > 200 mL/hour to facilitate sodium clearance
options.D: Potassium levels — hypertonic saline causes severe hypokalemia

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease and a known enteroenteric (intestine-to-intestine) fistula asks why they are losing weight despite eating normally. The nurse's most accurate explanation is:

options.A: "The Crohn's disease causes vomiting that prevents food absorption."
options.B: "The fistula creates a bypass between two intestinal segments, causing food to skip absorptive sections of the bowel. This, combined with active inflammation impairing nutrient absorption, chronic protein loss, and increased metabolic demands from inflammation, causes malnutrition despite adequate caloric intake."
options.C: "Your antidepressants are reducing your appetite."
options.D: "This is expected and will resolve once inflammation is controlled."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with severe GERD and obesity has failed PPI therapy, lifestyle modifications, and LNF surgery. Which advanced intervention might be considered, and what teaching would the nurse provide?

options.A: A second LNF surgery — the first likely failed due to poor technique
options.B: Bariatric surgery (such as Roux-en-Y gastric bypass) addresses both obesity and GERD simultaneously by reducing gastric volume, redirecting the gastric outlet, and significantly reducing acid reflux
options.C: Permanent NG tube placement for nutrition management
options.D: Permanent acid suppression via vagotomy

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is providing care for a client with PUD on bismuth-containing quadruple therapy. Which client statement indicates the need for additional teaching?

options.A: "My stools may appear black from the bismuth — I understand this is expected."
options.B: "I should take the tetracycline with dairy products to improve absorption."
options.C: "I need to avoid alcohol while taking metronidazole."
options.D: "I need to complete the full 10–14 day course even if my symptoms improve."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with BPH has been on finasteride for 6 months. PSA has dropped from 4.2 to 2.1 ng/mL. The provider notes the adjusted PSA (doubling the result) is 4.2 ng/mL and orders a prostate biopsy. The client is upset, saying "My PSA went down — why do I need a biopsy?" The nurse's best explanation is:

options.A: "The provider made an error — your PSA dropped, which means you are healthy."
options.B: "Finasteride reduces PSA by approximately 50%. To interpret your PSA accurately on finasteride, the result must be doubled. Your adjusted PSA is 4.2 ng/mL — the same as before treatment — meaning the expected PSA reduction from finasteride has NOT occurred. This lack of expected decrease raises concern for prostate cancer, which can maintain PSA despite finasteride."
options.C: "PSA monitoring is a routine test and the biopsy is just a precaution."
options.D: "Your PSA result was mislabeled in the lab."

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the post-operative plan for a client following right hemicolectomy for ruptured appendix with localized peritonitis. The client has a Jackson-Pratt drain in place. The drain output over 12 hours is 250 mL of serosanguineous fluid, decreasing from 350 mL during the previous shift. The nurse's interpretation is:

options.A: The decreasing drain output is a concerning sign of obstruction
options.B: Decreasing serosanguineous drain output is consistent with expected post-operative healing — inflammation and bleeding are decreasing. Continue monitoring for purulent, bilious, or sudden increases in output
options.C: The drain should be irrigated with NS to ensure patency
options.D: The drain should be removed since output is below 300 mL

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client admitted with shingles involving the right T4–T6 dermatome reports chest pain. The nurse's priority action is:

options.A: Reassure the client that the chest pain is from the shingles rash involving the chest dermatome
options.B: Obtain a 12-lead ECG and notify the provider before attributing the chest pain to shingles — shingles pain can mimic acute coronary syndrome and cardiac etiology must be ruled out first, especially in older adults
options.C: Administer a topical anesthetic to the rash area
options.D: Apply a heating pad to the chest to relieve the dermatome pain

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with inflammatory bowel disease is being started on azathioprine (an immunomodulator). The nurse includes which critical teaching points? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The therapeutic effect takes 3–6 months to develop fully
options.B: CBC must be monitored regularly for bone marrow suppression (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia)
options.C: The medication carries an increased risk of lymphoma with long-term use
options.D: The medication begins working immediately and can replace steroids within 48 hours
options.E: Report any signs of infection immediately since immune surveillance is reduced

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client is receiving TPN and the central venous catheter has been in place for 14 days. The nurse notes the following during assessment: temperature 103.2°F (new), chills, HR 118, blood cultures drawn 2 days ago are positive for Candida albicans. The nurse's priority action is:

options.A: Administer acetaminophen for fever and continue TPN
options.B: Notify the provider immediately — Candida fungemia from a central line is a lifethreatening complication. The CVC must likely be removed and antifungal therapy (e.g., fluconazole or echinocandin) initiated urgently. Fungal biofilms cannot be cleared with antibiotics alone
options.C: Flush the central line with antifungal solution
options.D: Change the TPN tubing and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the mechanism by which H. pylori causes peptic ulcer disease. Which statement most accurately describes the pathophysiology?

options.A: H. pylori directly dissolves the gastric mucosa with acid-like secretions
options.B: H. pylori uses urease to split urea into ammonia and CO₂, creating an alkaline microenvironment that allows it to survive in gastric acid; it then triggers chronic inflammation that degrades the protective mucus layer, allowing acid to attack the underlying mucosa
options.C: H. pylori stimulates the proton pump to produce excessive HCl
options.D: H. pylori causes autoimmune destruction of parietal cells

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client is post-op day 6 following exploratory laparotomy for peritonitis. The client has been on TPN and now has return of bowel sounds and passage of flatus. The provider orders to begin clear liquids. Which nursing assessment confirms readiness to advance the diet?

options.A: The client's blood glucose is within normal limits
options.B: Presence of bowel sounds in all four quadrants, passage of flatus, absence of nausea/vomiting, and tolerating sips of water without distress — all indicating return of bowel function
options.C: The client's WBC has normalized
options.D: The surgical incision is fully healed

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the care of a client with Crohn's disease who has developed short bowel syndrome following multiple intestinal resections. Which nutritional considerations are most appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Long-term or permanent TPN may be required if insufficient absorptive bowel length remains
options.B: Oral supplementation of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) since fat absorption is impaired
options.C: Vitamin B12 injections are required since the terminal ileum (absorption site) has been resected
options.D: A high-fiber, high-residue diet to stimulate the remaining bowel
options.E: Fluid and electrolyte supplementation to compensate for increased intestinal losses

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with meningococcal meningitis in the ICU is found to have bilateral adrenal hemorrhage on CT scan. The nurse recognizes this as:

options.A: An incidental finding unrelated to the meningococcal infection
options.B: Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome — bilateral adrenal gland destruction from meningococcal septicemia, causing acute adrenal insufficiency (cortisol deficiency), cardiovascular collapse, and dramatically elevated mortality
options.C: A side effect of mannitol therapy
options.D: Expected finding after LP procedure in meningitis

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with a history of GERD, smoking, obesity, and alcohol use develops progressively worsening dysphagia over 3 months. The dysphagia has progressed from solids to liquids. The client has lost 18 lbs unintentionally. The nurse's priority concern is:

options.A: The client's GERD medications are causing esophageal narrowing
options.B: Progressive dysphagia from solids to liquids combined with significant unintentional weight loss raises high concern for esophageal malignancy — this client requires urgent EGD with biopsy and provider notification
options.C: The client likely has esophageal spasm requiring nitrate therapy
options.D: The weight loss is from decreased appetite due to GERD discomfort

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is preparing a client for emergency surgery for a sigmoid volvulus. The client has the following ABG: pH 7.18, PaCO₂ 28 mmHg, HCO₃ 8 mEq/L, lactate 9.8 mmol/L. The nurse's interpretation is:

options.A: Compensated metabolic alkalosis — surgery can be briefly delayed
options.B: Severe metabolic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation AND critically elevated lactate (> 4 mmol/L = severe lactic acidosis) — this indicates massive bowel ischemia/necrosis with impending multi-organ failure; emergent surgery cannot be delayed
options.C: Respiratory acidosis from pre-operative anxiety and hyperventilation
options.D: Normal acid-base status for a preoperative client

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: A client with severe UC has been on high-dose IV corticosteroids for 7 days without improvement. The provider considers starting cyclosporine as rescue therapy. The nurse's pretreatment assessments should include: (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Serum creatinine and BUN — cyclosporine is nephrotoxic
options.B: Blood pressure — cyclosporine causes hypertension
options.C: Serum magnesium — cyclosporine causes hypomagnesemia
options.D: Serum cholesterol — cyclosporine can cause hyperlipidemia
options.E: Stool cultures to rule out infection before further immunosuppression

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client's diagnostic results. Which combination of findings is consistent with a diagnosis of Crohn's disease rather than UC? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Biopsy showing transmural inflammation with non-caseating granulomas
options.B: Colonoscopy showing continuous mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum
options.C: Imaging showing fistula formation and skip lesions with cobblestoning
options.D: Terminal ileum involvement on small bowel follow-through

questions.rationale: Rationale: Refer to your study guide and course materials for this question.

questions.text: Using NGN clinical judgment, which hypothesis best explains the entire clinical picture?

options.A: Severe GERD exacerbation with esophageal erosion
options.B: Perforated peptic ulcer with peritonitis and hemorrhagic shock — the "pain improvement" is perforation relieving ulcer pressure temporarily, while shock and rigidity indicate peritoneal contamination with hemodynamic compromise
options.C: Acute pancreatitis from NSAID use
options.D: Mallory-Weiss tear from a recent vomiting episode

questions.rationale: Respiratory rate of 10 Rationale: Hold hydromorphone if RR <12 breaths/minute due to risk of respiratory depression. This is a critical safety parameter for opioid administration.

questions.text: For the client in Scenario A, which interventions should the nurse initiate immediately and simultaneously? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Establish two large-bore IV lines (16-gauge or larger)
options.B: Infuse 0.9% NS or Lactated Ringer's wide open while awaiting blood products
options.C: Keep the client NPO and position flat or with legs elevated if hypotensive
options.D: Administer high-dose oral pantoprazole immediately
options.E: Draw STAT labs: CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type and crossmatch F. Notify the surgeon and prepare for emergent OR

questions.rationale: Naloxone Rationale: Naloxone (Narcan) is the opioid reversal agent and must be readily available when administering opioids. It reverses respiratory depression.

questions.text: The client in Scenario A is taken to the OR for an exploratory laparotomy. Post-operatively on day 2, his blood glucose is 480 mg/dL. His ABG shows pH 7.22, PaCO₂ 24 mmHg, HCO₃ 9 mEq/L. Serum ketones are elevated. The nurse's clinical judgment identifies this as:

options.A: Expected post-surgical acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion
options.B: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) precipitated by the physiological stress of emergency surgery — stress hormones (cortisol, glucagon, epinephrine) drive severe hyperglycemia and ketogenesis, particularly in a client with pre-existing type 2 diabetes; this requires emergent endocrinology input, insulin drip, and IV fluid/electrolyte management
options.C: Lactic acidosis from continued bowel ischemia
options.D: Respiratory alkalosis from post-operative pain

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Verify dose, Monitor RR, Have naloxone, Assess LOC, Monitor BP Rationale: Hydromorphone is a high-alert medication requiring double-checks, respiratory monitoring, reversal agent availability, and comprehensive assessment.

questions.text: On post-operative day 4 of Scenario A, the client develops a temperature of 104.2°F, HR 126, BP 94/60 mmHg, RR 26/min, and increasing abdominal distension despite NG tube. WBC is 29,000/mm³ and lactate is 5.4 mmol/L. Which priority nursing diagnosis is most appropriate?

options.A: Acute pain related to surgical incision
options.B: Ineffective tissue perfusion related to septic shock from peritonitis, evidenced by hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate, and fever
options.C: Risk for aspiration related to NG tube placement
options.D: Deficient fluid volume related to post-operative NPO status

questions.rationale: Increase fluids and fiber—expected side effect Rationale: Constipation is an expected side effect of opioids. Management includes increased fluids, fiber, and stool softeners—not medication discontinuation.

questions.text: The client in Scenario A has now developed ARDS (acute respiratory distress syndrome) as a complication of septic shock. His PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio is 88 (severe ARDS < 100). He is intubated. Which ventilator strategy is most consistent with ARDS management?

options.A: High tidal volume ventilation (12–15 mL/kg ideal body weight) to ensure full lung inflation
options.B: Lung-protective ventilation: low tidal volume (6 mL/kg ideal body weight), permissive hypercapnia, PEEP titration to prevent alveolar collapse and minimize barotrauma
options.C: Continuous high-flow oxygen via nasal cannula at 15 L/min
options.D: Prone positioning is contraindicated in post-abdominal surgery clients

questions.rationale: Magnesium and B12 levels Rationale: Long-term PPI use can cause hypomagnesemia and vitamin B12 deficiency. These should be monitored periodically.

questions.text: The client in Scenario A recovers and is being discharged. Which comprehensive discharge teaching plan reflects multi-system management of all his conditions? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Absolutely avoid NSAIDs — substitute acetaminophen (with provider guidance) or discuss non-pharmacological arthritis management
options.B: Maintain a low-fat, GERD-appropriate diet; elevate head of bed
options.C: Complete the full H. pylori eradication therapy course if H. pylori was confirmed
options.D: Take pantoprazole as prescribed — do not stop when symptoms improve
options.E: Monitor blood glucose daily and follow up with endocrinology for diabetes management F. Return to emergency department for any recurrence of severe epigastric pain, hematemesis, or dizziness SCENARIO B — Questions 7–11 A 22-year-old college student is brought to the ED by roommates. She is confused, has a temperature of 104.8°F, severe headache rated 10/10, photophobia, phonophobia, stiff neck, and a rapidly spreading petechial rash covering her trunk and lower extremities. The rash is nonblanching. BP is 92/58 mmHg, HR 138 bpm.

questions.rationale: Hypomagnesemia Rationale: Long-term PPI use can cause hypomagnesemia, which manifests as muscle cramps, tetany, and cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.text: Using the NGN Clinical Judgment Model, the nurse's immediate recognition step identifies this presentation as:

options.A: Viral meningitis with febrile seizure
options.B: Meningococcal meningitis with septicemia (purpura fulminans) — the non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash, hypotension, tachycardia, and meningeal signs together constitute a true medical emergency with potential death within hours
options.C: Severe migraine with autonomic instability
options.D: Encephalitis from herpes simplex virus

questions.rationale: May indicate C. diff—notify provider Rationale: PPIs increase risk of C. difficile infection. Severe diarrhea with cramping should raise suspicion for this serious complication.

questions.text: Before any other action, which intervention takes absolute priority for the client in Scenario B?

options.A: Perform a lumbar puncture immediately to confirm diagnosis
options.B: Obtain a CT scan of the head before any other intervention
options.C: Establish IV access, draw blood cultures, and initiate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) within minutes — in suspected meningococcemia, every minute of antibiotic delay increases mortality. Do NOT delay for LP or CT
options.D: Administer IV acetaminophen for fever reduction first

questions.rationale: Dose adjustment for renal impairment Rationale: Famotidine is renally cleared and requires dose adjustment in clients with renal impairment to prevent accumulation.

questions.text: The client in Scenario B has a documented penicillin anaphylaxis. Ceftriaxone is ordered. The most appropriate provider decision, explained to the nursing team, is:

options.A: The risk of withholding antibiotics in rapidly progressive meningococcemia far outweighs the cross-reactivity risk with ceftriaxone. Administer ceftriaxone with emergency allergy equipment at bedside — anaphylaxis is treatable but untreated meningococcemia has >20% mortality
options.B: Withhold all cephalosporins and wait for allergy consultation
options.C: Administer azithromycin instead — equally effective for meningococcal meningitis
options.D: Administer a penicillin-based drug at a reduced dose since the anaphylaxis was "years ago"

questions.rationale: Potential side effect—assess and notify Rationale: Confusion is a known side effect of H2 blockers in elderly clients, possibly due to reduced hepatic clearance.

questions.text: Over 2 hours in Scenario B, the client's rash evolves to large purpuric plaques, fingertips become cyanotic and cold, BP drops to 72/40 mmHg despite 2L IV fluids, urine output is 5 mL/hour, and platelet count falls to 18,000/mm³. PT/PTT are markedly prolonged. The nurse recognizes the client has developed two simultaneous life-threatening complications. Identify both: (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Septic shock from meningococcal septicemia
options.B: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.C: Acute respiratory distress syndrome
options.D: Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage)
options.E: Hypovolemic shock from GI bleeding

questions.rationale: Take 2 hours before or after omeprazole Rationale: Sucralfate binds to other medications and impairs their absorption. Separate all medications by at least 2 hours.

questions.text: The client in Scenario B survives after ICU care. At discharge, both lower legs have been amputated due to gangrene from ischemic necrosis. The nurse's discharge planning must include which priority interventions? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Referral to prosthetics and rehabilitation medicine
options.B: Psychological support/counseling — catastrophic body image change in a 22-year-old
options.C: Education for the client and family about ongoing infection risk and future meningococcal vaccination for contacts
options.D: Arrange follow-up for hearing assessment — meningitis causes sensorineural hearing loss
options.E: No further follow-up is needed since the infection has been treated SCENARIO C — Questions 12–16 A 67-year-old female with a 20-year history of Crohn's disease, multiple prior bowel resections (including terminal ileum), and current infliximab therapy is admitted with fever of 103°F, severe RLQ pain, and a palpable tender mass. CT scan shows a 5 cm intraabdominal abscess in the RLQ. She is also confused, with HR 118 and BP 98/64.

questions.rationale: Empty stomach, separate from other meds by 2 hours Rationale: Sucralfate works by coating the ulcer and must be given on an empty stomach. It impairs absorption of many drugs.

questions.text: The nurse's priority hypothesis for this client's clinical picture is:

options.A: Crohn's disease exacerbation requiring mesalamine adjustment
options.B: Intraabdominal abscess progressing to sepsis from Crohn's disease, complicated by immunosuppression from infliximab — requires urgent surgical/interventional radiology drainage, IV antibiotics, and sepsis bundle activation
options.C: Bowel obstruction from prior adhesions requiring NG decompression
options.D: UTI causing systemic symptoms in an elderly patient

questions.rationale: Fever/bloody diarrhea, C. diff, Infectious gastroenteritis Rationale: Loperamide is contraindicated in infectious diarrhea as it slows motility and traps pathogens, potentially worsening the infection.

questions.text: The infliximab must be addressed in the care of the client in Scenario C. Which clinical decision is most accurate?

options.A: Continue infliximab to control the Crohn's inflammation during the infection
options.B: Hold infliximab immediately — administering anti-TNF therapy during an active serious infection (abscess/sepsis) dramatically increases the risk of overwhelming, uncontrolled infection. Infliximab must be withheld until the infection is fully resolved
options.C: Reduce the infliximab dose by half and continue
options.D: Replace infliximab with mesalamine during the infection episode

questions.rationale: Do NOT exceed dose, avoid with fever/blood Rationale: Loperamide overdose can cause serious cardiac effects. Never use with signs of infectious diarrhea.

questions.text: The client in Scenario C has a history of multiple terminal ileal resections. Her labs show: Hgb 7.4 g/dL, MCV 108 fL (macrocytic), B12 level 78 pg/mL (critically low). Which interventions are indicated? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Oral B12 supplementation — oral B12 is adequate replacement
options.B: Intramuscular B12 (cyanocobalamin) injections — since the terminal ileum is resected, oral B12 cannot be absorbed (intrinsic factor binding occurs here); IM administration bypasses this
options.C: Folic acid supplementation — macrocytic anemia may also involve folate deficiency, especially with Crohn's malabsorption and methotrexate use
options.D: Iron supplementation for microcytic anemia
options.E: Evaluate for pernicious anemia and intrinsic factor antibodies

questions.rationale: Do NOT crush extended-release Rationale: Crushing destroys the delayed-release mechanism, causing medication to be released in the wrong part of the GI tract.

questions.text: After successful abscess drainage and 10 days of IV antibiotics, the team re-evaluates the client in Scenario C. The Crohn's disease has been silent for 3 years on infliximab. The team considers restarting infliximab. The nurse educates the client: (Select all that apply.)

options.A: TB re-screening is required before restarting infliximab
options.B: The client must be current on all vaccinations — all live vaccines (e.g., live attenuated influenza, live zoster) must be given before resuming biologic therapy, as live vaccines are contraindicated while on biologics
options.C: Regular monitoring for malignancy is required during biologic therapy
options.D: Infliximab can be restarted immediately upon discharge before infection is confirmed cleared
options.E: Signs of infection must be reported promptly — any fever, productive cough, night sweats, or weight loss requires evaluation

questions.rationale: Possible blood dyscrasias—notify provider Rationale: Mesalamine rarely causes bone marrow suppression. Sore throat, fever, and bruising are warning signs requiring immediate evaluation.

questions.text: The client in Scenario C develops short bowel syndrome following this hospitalization, requiring long-term home TPN. Which statement by the client during discharge teaching demonstrates correct understanding of home TPN management?

options.A: "I can stop the TPN whenever I feel hungry and want to eat instead."
options.B: "I will check my blood glucose at the start, middle, and end of each infusion cycle; I will call the nurse if I develop fever or chills; I will change the TPN tubing and IV dressing as instructed; and I understand I must taper the TPN — never stop it abruptly."
options.C: "Since TPN is a food supplement, I can pause it anytime without risk."
options.D: "I only need to check my blood glucose if I feel shaky."

questions.rationale: Renal function, CBC, Liver function Rationale: Mesalamine can affect renal function and rarely causes blood dyscrasias. Regular monitoring is recommended.

questions.text: A client with BPH undergoes urodynamic testing, which shows detrusor overactivity with bladder outlet obstruction. The provider orders solifenacin (an anticholinergic) for overactive bladder. The nurse identifies which critical concern with this medication in the context of BPH?

options.A: Solifenacin and finasteride interact causing dangerous hypertension
options.B: Anticholinergics can worsen urinary retention in clients with BPH by relaxing the detrusor muscle and increasing bladder outlet resistance — in a client with alreadycompromised urine flow, this can precipitate acute urinary retention. The risk-benefit must be carefully discussed with the provider
options.C: Solifenacin causes gynecomastia when combined with finasteride
options.D: Solifenacin cannot be used with any BPH medications

questions.rationale: Penicillins and Cephalosporins Rationale: Cross-reactivity exists between penicillins and cephalosporins. Assess for allergy to both classes before administration.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client who has been treated for GERD with once-daily omeprazole for 5 years without reassessment. The client now presents with new dysphagia, 15-lb weight loss, and iron deficiency anemia (Hgb 9.1). Which statement reflects the highest level of clinical judgment?

options.A: Increase the omeprazole dose since symptoms indicate inadequate acid suppression
options.B: The combination of new dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, and iron deficiency anemia in a long-standing GERD client is an alarm symptom cluster requiring urgent EGD — these findings raise significant concern for esophageal adenocarcinoma. The nurse must notify the provider immediately
options.C: Recommend dietary modifications and reassess in 3 months
options.D: Start empirical antibiotics for H. pylori without testing

questions.rationale: Calcium-containing solutions Rationale: Ceftriaxone can precipitate when mixed with calcium-containing solutions, especially dangerous in neonates.

questions.text: A client with viral gastroenteritis from Norovirus is in a shared hospital room. The roommate is a 78-year-old immunocompromised client post-chemotherapy. Which nursing action best demonstrates infection control priority?

options.A: Place a sign on the door reminding staff to wash hands
options.B: Immediately request a private room for the Norovirus client, implement contact precautions, and use soap-and-water handwashing — Norovirus is highly contagious and alcohol sanitizer is ineffective. The immunocompromised roommate faces life-threatening risk from exposure
options.C: Ask the Norovirus client to wear a mask
options.D: Cohorting is acceptable since both clients are already hospitalized

questions.rationale: C. difficile infection Rationale: Antibiotic-associated C. difficile colitis is a serious complication of ceftriaxone and other broad-spectrum antibiotics.

questions.text: A nurse is providing post-operative teaching for a 52-year-old male following TURP. The client becomes tearful and states: "I read online that I might never be able to ejaculate normally again." The nurse's most therapeutic and clinically accurate response is:

options.A: "Those websites are wrong — TURP has no sexual side effects."
options.B: "Retrograde ejaculation is a common outcome of TURP — semen travels backward into the bladder instead of outward during orgasm. This does not cause harm or affect sexual pleasure or the ability to have an erection, but it does affect fertility. Your urine may appear cloudy after orgasm. This is an important conversation to have with your provider if fertility is a concern."
options.C: "You should have discussed this before the surgery — nothing can be done now."
options.D: "Retrograde ejaculation is temporary and will resolve within 6 weeks."

questions.rationale: QT prolongation on ECG Rationale: Azithromycin can prolong the QT interval, increasing risk of fatal arrhythmias, especially in clients with cardiac history.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a medication reconciliation for a hospitalized client with PUD, meningitis, and concurrent BPH. The client's home medication list includes: omeprazole, ceftriaxone (started in hospital), prazosin, finasteride, and ibuprofen. Which medication on this list requires immediate action?

options.A: Omeprazole — interacts with ceftriaxone
options.B: Prazosin — causes fluid retention worsening meningitis
options.C: Ibuprofen — NSAIDs worsen peptic ulcers, directly antagonize the ulcer treatment, and increase risk of GI hemorrhage. Must be discontinued immediately and provider notified
options.D: Finasteride — contraindicated with ceftriaxone

questions.rationale: Complete course, Report palpitations, Avoid sun, GI upset Rationale: Important teaching points. Do NOT take with antacids as they interfere with absorption.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a new graduate's charting on a client with peritonitis. The new graduate documented: "Client's abdomen has board-like rigidity — administered ketorolac IV as ordered for pain." Which error in the documentation requires immediate follow-up with the new graduate?

options.A: Ketorolac should have been morphine for this level of pain
options.B: Ketorolac (an NSAID) is potentially contraindicated in a client with peritonitis who is at high risk for stress ulcers and GI perforation complications — administering an NSAID in this context could worsen mucosal injury. This requires review with the provider and correction of the practice
options.C: The documentation should have included pain rating before and after
options.D: There is no error — ketorolac is always appropriate for abdominal pain

questions.rationale: Take 2 hours before OR 6 hours after calcium Rationale: Calcium, iron, and antacids chelate (bind) ciprofloxacin and dramatically reduce absorption.

questions.text: The nurse is performing a bow-tie clinical judgment assessment. A client presents with: jaundice, clay-colored stools, dark urine, RUQ pain radiating to right shoulder, fever of 103.2°F, and rigors. Their WBC is 24,000. Which two conditions in the differential require urgent surgical/procedural intervention rather than conservative management?

options.A: Simple cholecystitis and uncomplicated GERD
options.B: Ascending cholangitis (biliary obstruction with infection) and gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction — both require emergent ERCP or surgical drainage; conservative management risks septic shock and death
options.C: Viral hepatitis and chronic cholecystitis
options.D: Appendicitis and sigmoid volvulus

questions.rationale: Discontinue, notify provider, rest—tendon rupture risk Rationale: Fluoroquinolones can cause tendon rupture. Any tendon pain requires immediate medication discontinuation and rest.

questions.text: A client with shingles on the face and scalp develops sudden onset of severe vertigo, hearing loss, facial paralysis (left side), and vesicles in the left ear canal. The nurse recognizes this as Ramsay Hunt Syndrome and understands that the priority clinical concern beyond the immediate symptoms is:

options.A: Risk of shingles spreading to the right side of the face
options.B: Without aggressive antiviral therapy, permanent facial nerve paralysis (Bell's palsy) and permanent sensorineural hearing loss are likely outcomes. Early IV acyclovir and steroids significantly improve neurological recovery rates
options.C: The risk of meningitis from cranial nerve involvement
options.D: The client needs immediate ENT surgery to release facial nerve pressure

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Calcium, Iron, Antacids, Dairy, Multivitamins Rationale: All minerals and antacids bind fluoroquinolones. Separate by 2 hours before or 6 hours after.

questions.text: A nurse is precepting a new graduate who states: "I think we should give the patient with ulcerative colitis a high-fiber diet to improve their bowel function." The preceptor nurse's most accurate clinical correction is:

options.A: "You are correct — fiber always improves bowel function."
options.B: "High-fiber diets are contraindicated during UC flares. Insoluble fiber increases bowel motility and stimulation, worsening diarrhea and cramping. The correct dietary approach during a flare is low-residue, low-fiber, lactose-free — minimizing GI stimulation. Fiber may be gradually reintroduced during remission."
options.C: "Fiber is only contraindicated if the client has a bowel obstruction."
options.D: "Fiber recommendations are the same for all IBD conditions."

questions.rationale: Tendon rupture, CNS effects, QT prolongation, Hypoglycemia, Neuropathy Rationale: Elderly clients are at higher risk for fluoroquinolone toxicity including multiple serious adverse effects.

questions.text: The nurse reviews the following arterial blood gas results for a client with severe gastroenteritis who has been vomiting profusely for 4 days: pH 7.58, PaCO₂ 50 mmHg, HCO₃ 44 mEq/L. The nurse interprets this as:

options.A: Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation
options.B: Metabolic alkalosis from profound loss of HCl through vomiting (loss of H⁺ and Cl⁻) with compensatory respiratory retention of CO₂ (hypoventilation to raise PaCO₂ and return pH toward normal) — requires IV fluid with potassium chloride replacement
options.C: Uncompensated metabolic acidosis
options.D: Mixed acid-base disorder

questions.rationale: Take first dose at BEDTIME Rationale: First-dose syncope is a known effect of alpha-blockers. Bedtime dosing allows the BP drop to occur while lying down.

questions.text: The nurse is evaluating a client with refeeding syndrome developing after TPN was initiated following a prolonged bowel obstruction. Labs show: phosphate 1.2 mg/dL (critically low), K⁺ 2.8 mEq/L, Mg²⁺ 1.0 mEq/L. ECG shows prolonged QT interval. The nurse recognizes this as:

options.A: Expected electrolyte changes from TPN initiation
options.B: Refeeding syndrome — rapid reinstitution of carbohydrate-based nutrition drives insulin release, which shifts phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly, causing critical extracellular depletion. The QT prolongation from hypophosphatemia and hypomagnesemia places the client at risk for fatal ventricular arrhythmias
options.C: Hypovolemia from inadequate TPN fluid delivery
options.D: TPN-associated hepatic steatosis affecting electrolyte metabolism

questions.rationale: Alcohol, Hot showers, Standing quickly, Prolonged standing Rationale: These activities worsen orthostatic hypotension. Adequate fluids help maintain blood volume.

questions.text: (continued) The nurse is performing a focused assessment using the NGN bowtie format for a client with active PUD bleeding. Client cues: hematemesis + BP 84/52 + HR 132 + cool clammy skin + altered mental status. Which priority action cluster correctly reflects the nurse's response?

options.A: Administer oral pantoprazole, place in Semi-Fowler's, call the provider in the morning
options.B: Establish two large-bore IVs, infuse isotonic crystalloid wide open, place client flat with legs elevated, draw STAT H&H/coagulation/type & crossmatch, notify provider and surgeon emergently, prepare for blood transfusion and endoscopy — this client is in hemorrhagic shock
options.C: Administer IV antiemetic, reposition client, and recheck BP in 30 minutes
options.D: Insert NG tube, irrigate with ice saline, and discharge when BP improves

questions.rationale: Lying, sitting, then standing with intervals Rationale: Proper orthostatic BP assessment requires sequential measurements in different positions with 1-minute intervals.

questions.text: A client with meningitis is receiving IV dexamethasone. The nurse notes the client's blood glucose rises from 112 to 298 mg/dL over 24 hours. The nurse's clinical reasoning identifies:

options.A: The meningitis organism is causing hyperglycemia directly
options.B: Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) antagonize insulin and stimulate gluconeogenesis, causing steroid-induced hyperglycemia — a known and expected adverse effect requiring glucose monitoring every 4–6 hours and insulin coverage as ordered; the provider must be informed of the glucose trend
options.C: The client has developed new-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus from the infection
options.D: The hyperglycemia is from the IV dextrose in the antibiotic carrier solution only

questions.rationale: Pregnant women must NOT handle tablets Rationale: Finasteride can be absorbed through skin and cause abnormal genital development in male fetuses.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a care scenario involving a client post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy who is on the medical-surgical floor. The charge nurse is assigning care. Which client assignment is most appropriate for a new graduate nurse (less than 3 months experience)?

options.A: The client with ascending cholangitis progressing to septic shock requiring vasopressors
options.B: The client post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy day 1, pain well-controlled at 2/10, tolerating sips, no complications — stable and predictable
options.C: The client with toxic megacolon requiring emergency colectomy preparation
options.D: The client with TURP syndrome showing sodium of 118 mEq/L and confusion

questions.rationale: Within 3-6 months as prostate shrinks Rationale: Finasteride works by gradually shrinking the prostate through blocking DHT conversion. This takes months.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease on long-term prednisone therapy asks: "Why do I keep getting infections and my bones hurt?" The nurse's most clinically comprehensive explanation addresses which adverse effects? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Long-term corticosteroids suppress the immune system, impairing the body's ability to fight infections (bacterial, fungal, viral)
options.B: Corticosteroids accelerate bone resorption and impair calcium absorption, causing steroid-induced osteoporosis and fracture risk
options.C: Corticosteroids cause cataracts, adrenal suppression, and hyperglycemia with prolonged use
options.D: Corticosteroids strengthen muscles and bones with long-term use
options.E: Corticosteroids should never be stopped abruptly — the adrenal glands atrophy during long-term therapy and cannot produce cortisol rapidly if the drug is stopped suddenly (adrenal crisis)

questions.rationale: Double the value (to 5.0 ng/mL) Rationale: Finasteride lowers PSA by approximately 50%. To accurately screen for prostate cancer, double the PSA value.

questions.text: The nurse is performing a quality improvement review. A client on the unit developed a central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) while receiving TPN. Which systemlevel failures most likely contributed to this outcome? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: TPN tubing was changed every 48 hours instead of every 24 hours
options.B: The CVC dressing was left unchanged for 10 days (should be every 5–7 days for transparent dressing)
options.C: Blood glucose was monitored too frequently
options.D: Sterile technique was not consistently maintained during access
options.E: The TPN was administered through a peripheral IV rather than a central line

questions.rationale: Document and notify—gynecomastia is known side effect Rationale: Gynecomastia is a known side effect of finasteride due to altered testosterone/estrogen balance.

questions.text: A client with UC is post-op following emergent total colectomy for toxic megacolon. The client has a new ileostomy. On post-op day 2, the ileostomy output is 2,800 mL in 24 hours (high output). Labs show Na⁺ 128 mEq/L, K⁺ 2.6 mEq/L. The nurse's priority interventions include: (Select all that apply.)

options.A: IV fluid replacement with electrolyte-containing solutions to correct losses
options.B: Notify the provider — high-output ileostomy with hyponatremia and hypokalemia requires aggressive electrolyte management and possible loperamide to slow output
options.C: Restrict all fluid intake to prevent further electrolyte dilution
options.D: Monitor for signs of dehydration: hypotension, tachycardia, decreased skin turgor
options.E: Consult wound/ostomy/continence (WOC) nurse for high-output ileostomy management

questions.rationale: Hang D10W to prevent hypoglycemia Rationale: If TPN is unavailable, D10W prevents rebound hypoglycemia that occurs when high glucose infusion suddenly stops.

questions.text: A client with BPH, hypertension, and diabetes presents to the clinic. His current medications are: prazosin, finasteride, metformin, lisinopril, and sildenafil (for erectile dysfunction). The nurse identifies which critical drug interaction that requires immediate provider notification?

options.A: Metformin and finasteride — both affect glucose metabolism
options.B: Prazosin and sildenafil — both cause vasodilation and significant blood pressure lowering; combined use can cause severe, potentially fatal hypotension. This combination is generally contraindicated or requires extreme caution
options.C: Lisinopril and metformin — renal interaction in diabetic clients
options.D: Finasteride and sildenafil — hormonal interaction affecting erectile function

questions.rationale: Stop TPN, Draw cultures, Notify provider Rationale: Suspected line infection requires stopping the potential source, obtaining cultures, and immediate provider notification.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client with Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome admitted with refractory PUD. Labs show: gastrin level 1,200 pg/mL (normal < 100), secretin stimulation test causing a paradoxical increase in gastrin. Imaging identifies a 1.2 cm mass in the pancreatic head. The nurse understands the clinical implications: (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The tumor is a gastrinoma — it secretes gastrin autonomously, causing massive acid hypersecretion unresponsive to standard PPI doses
options.B: Gastrinomas are associated with MEN-1 syndrome (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1) and the client should be evaluated for parathyroid and pituitary tumors
options.C: Standard PPI therapy at normal doses is sufficient to control acid secretion
options.D: Octreotide (a somatostatin analog) may be used to reduce gastrin secretion from the tumor
options.E: Surgical resection of the gastrinoma is potentially curative if the tumor is localized and has not metastasized

questions.rationale: Gradually decrease TPN as enteral increases Rationale: Gradual transition prevents hypoglycemia and allows assessment of enteral tolerance.

questions.text: The nurse is completing a focused assessment using the NGN trend monitoring framework. A client with peritonitis is post-op day 5. Review the following shift data and identify which trend requires immediate escalation: Parameter Day 3 Day 4 Day 5 Temp (°F) 100.4 100.8 104.6 WBC (K/mm³) 14 16 29 BP (mmHg) 118/76 112/72 86/54 UO (mL/hr) 42 38 11 Mental status Alert Alert Confused

options.A: The temperature trend alone warrants a call to the provider
options.B: The combination of escalating fever, rapidly rising WBC, falling blood pressure, critically declining urine output, and new confusion represents a septic shock trajectory — this is a medical emergency requiring immediate rapid response activation, blood cultures, broadspectrum antibiotics, and fluid resuscitation
options.C: The data show normal post-operative variation — continue monitoring
options.D: Only the blood pressure drop requires provider notification

questions.rationale: Verify placement, Confirm X-ray, Check residual, Elevate HOB Rationale: All are essential before enteral feeding. Never feed with client flat as this increases aspiration risk.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client with a complex enterovesical fistula from Crohn's disease who has developed recurrent polymicrobial urinary tract infections with organisms including Bacteroides fragilis and E. coli. The nurse understands which management approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Long-term oral antibiotics alone will cure the fistula and prevent recurrent UTIs
options.B: Enterovesical fistulas require surgical repair to permanently eliminate the connection between the bowel and bladder — antibiotics treat active infection but do not close the fistula. Nutritional optimization, disease control (biologic therapy), and surgical planning are the comprehensive approach
options.C: The client should increase fluid intake to flush out the bacteria
options.D: Intermittent catheterization will prevent recurrent UTIs from the fistula

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Anticholinergics, Antihistamines, Decongestants, Antipsychotics, Muscle relaxants Rationale: These medication classes can worsen urinary retention in BPH. Beta-blockers are not typically problematic.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with shingles who asks: "My neighbor just had a kidney transplant and is on tacrolimus. Can I visit her?" The nurse's most clinically sound response is:

options.A: "Yes, visits are fine as long as you wash your hands."
options.B: "If your lesions are still open and weeping, you must not visit your neighbor. You can transmit varicella virus to your immunosuppressed neighbor, in whom primary varicella infection or VZV reactivation can be life-threatening. Wait until every lesion is fully crusted over — and your neighbor's transplant team should be notified of the potential exposure regardless."
options.C: "Shingles cannot be transmitted to adults, so the visit is safe."
options.D: "The visit is safe if your neighbor wears a mask."

questions.rationale: Avoid decongestants—worsen retention Rationale: Decongestants (alpha-agonists) constrict the bladder neck and urethra, worsening urinary retention.

questions.text: The nurse is providing end-of-shift handoff (SBAR) for a client with cholecystitis and early sepsis. Arrange the SBAR components in the correct order and identify the most critical "R" (Recommendation) from the following options:

options.A: Recommend reassessing at the next shift change — the client is stable
options.B: Recommend immediate escalation of IV antibiotics to broad-spectrum coverage, preparation for emergent ERCP or cholecystectomy, activation of the sepsis bundle (blood cultures, 30 mL/kg fluid bolus, lactate measurement), and ICU consultation given BP 88/56, HR 128, and fever of 104°F
options.C: Recommend oral antibiotics and discharge planning
options.D: Recommend pain management only and routine monitoring

questions.rationale: Large catheter presses on stretch receptors—normal Rationale: Post-TURP catheters are large (20-24 Fr) and stimulate bladder stretch receptors, causing a normal urge to void.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing end-of-life considerations for a client with stage IV meningococcal septicemia who has developed gangrene of both hands and feet and has been on vasopressors for 5 days with no improvement. The family asks: "Is there anything more that can be done?" The nurse's most appropriate response reflects:

options.A: "We can always add more antibiotics — there are always more options."
options.B: "We are doing everything possible. I'd like to suggest we arrange a care conference with the entire team, including palliative care, so that all your questions can be answered and we can make sure your loved one's comfort and dignity are the center of our care decisions."
options.C: "We should consider stopping all treatment immediately."
options.D: "Your family member will recover — just give the medications more time."

questions.rationale: 300 mL Rationale: Irrigation used: 3000-1200=1800 mL. True urine: 2100-1800=300 mL.

questions.text: A client with PUD is admitted for active GI bleeding. The nurse receives the following laboratory trend over 4 hours: Time Hgb BP HR 0800 9.8 g/dL 118/78 84 1000 8.2 g/dL 104/68 98 1200 6.4 g/dL 88/54 124 Using NGN trend recognition, the nurse identifies this as active hemorrhage with deteriorating hemodynamic compensation. Which complete priority action set is correct?

options.A: Recheck labs at 1400 and notify the provider if Hgb falls below 5.0 g/dL
options.B: Immediately notify the provider and surgeon, establish or confirm large-bore IV access × 2, transfuse PRBCs per order, prepare for emergent EGD, and activate massive transfusion protocol if the hemorrhage is uncontrolled
options.C: Administer oral iron supplements and increase IV PPI rate
options.D: Reposition client and administer IV fluids at 75 mL/hour

questions.rationale: 350 mL Rationale: True urine = Total drainage - Irrigation infused = 4350 - 4000 = 350 mL.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a clinical learning moment. A nursing student administered loperamide to a client with gastroenteritis who had a WBC of 18,000/mm³ and bloody stools, without assessing for contraindications. The nurse preceptor discusses which teaching points? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Assessment of stool characteristics (bloody vs. non-bloody) and temperature must precede any antidiarrheal administration
options.B: Loperamide is contraindicated in infectious diarrhea with fever and bloody stools — it traps toxins and bacteria, worsening the infection and risking toxic megacolon
options.C: The student should have verified the contraindications with the pharmacist or provider before administering
options.D: Loperamide is safe for all types of diarrhea in hospitalized clients
options.E: This situation should be documented as a medication error and reported through the incident reporting system

questions.rationale: Assess for obstruction and irrigate if ordered Rationale: First assess the system for kinks or clots. Gentle irrigation may be ordered to clear obstruction.

questions.text: The nurse is providing care for a client following total colectomy with J-pouch creation for UC. Six months post-reversal of the temporary ileostomy, the client reports: fever, increased stool frequency (15+ per day), watery stools, urgency, and pelvic discomfort. Labs show elevated CRP and ESR. The nurse suspects:

options.A: A recurrence of ulcerative colitis in the J-pouch
options.B: Pouchitis — inflammation of the ileal J-pouch, the most common long-term complication of IPAA surgery, treated with metronidazole or ciprofloxacin; the nurse should notify the provider and anticipate endoscopic evaluation of the pouch
options.C: Crohn's disease developing in the J-pouch
options.D: Post-surgical bowel obstruction from adhesions

questions.rationale: Red/blood-tinged → pink → light pink/clear Rationale: Normal post-TURP progression shows gradual clearing from blood-tinged to pink to clear as healing occurs.

questions.text: A client with a 30-year history of Crohn's disease develops a mass in the terminal ileum on colonoscopy. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma of the small bowel. The nurse provides the most accurate education when stating:

options.A: "Crohn's disease does not increase the risk of bowel cancer."
options.B: "Long-standing Crohn's disease, particularly with chronic transmural inflammation, increases the risk of small bowel and colorectal cancer. Regular surveillance colonoscopy is recommended starting 8–10 years after diagnosis, especially with extensive colonic involvement."
options.C: "This cancer is not related to your Crohn's disease."
options.D: "Colorectal cancer risk from Crohn's is the same as the general population."

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Mouth to anus, Skip lesions, Transmural, Cobblestone, Fistulas Rationale: These distinguish Crohn's from UC. UC has continuous inflammation starting from the rectum (option D).

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing an NGN-style matrix question. For each client condition listed, identify whether the priority intervention is Surgical, Medical, or Both (requires simultaneous medical stabilization AND surgical planning): Condition Priority Perforated peptic ulcer with peritonitis ? Toxic megacolon not responding to 72-hour medical therapy ? Ascending cholangitis with septic shock ? Sigmoid volvulus with ischemic bowel ? Uncomplicated acute appendicitis ?

options.A: All conditions are medical — surgery should be the last resort
options.B: Perforated PUD = Both (resuscitate then OR); Toxic megacolon = Both (medical stabilization then emergency colectomy); Ascending cholangitis = Both (antibiotics + emergent ERCP/surgical drainage); Sigmoid volvulus with ischemia = Both (urgent resuscitation then emergency OR); Appendicitis = Surgical (laparoscopic appendectomy)
options.C: All conditions are surgical — no medical management is needed
options.D: Only toxic megacolon and sigmoid volvulus are surgical — all others are medical

questions.rationale: A, B, D, E, F — Skin care, High-cal/protein, Wound specialist, Pouching, Infection monitoring Rationale: Fistula management is comprehensive. LOW protein (option C) is incorrect—high protein supports healing.

questions.text: A client with meningitis is on IV ceftriaxone and IV dexamethasone. The nurse is educating a nursing student about timing. The student asks: "Does it matter when we give the dexamethasone relative to the antibiotic?" The nurse's most accurate teaching is:

options.A: "No — timing doesn't matter for either drug."
options.B: "Dexamethasone should ideally be given 15–20 minutes BEFORE or with the first dose of antibiotics. When antibiotics kill bacteria, bacterial cell walls rupture and release inflammatory mediators (endotoxins). Pre-treating with steroids blunts this cytokine surge before it peaks. Giving steroids after the first antibiotic dose significantly reduces the neuroprotective benefit."
options.C: "Dexamethasone should be given 2 hours after the antibiotic to avoid interaction."
options.D: "Dexamethasone is given only if the client develops seizures — not routinely."

questions.rationale: Terminal ileum commonly affected Rationale: B12-intrinsic factor complex is absorbed in the terminal ileum. Crohn's commonly affects this area.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client who is 12 hours post-TURP. The CBI is infusing. The nurse notes the following hourly outputs for the last 3 hours: Hour CBI In Total Drain Out True UO 1 300 mL 280 mL ? 2 300 mL 310 mL ? 3 300 mL 120 mL ? The nurse calculates true urine output for each hour and identifies which finding requires immediate action:

options.A: Hour 1: −20 mL is the concern (drainage less than input)
options.B: Hour 3: True UO = −180 mL (far less output than irrigation input), suggesting catheter obstruction by a blood clot — the nurse must assess the catheter for patency, attempt to dislodge the clot by increasing CBI, and notify the provider if catheter obstruction is confirmed
options.C: Hour 2: +10 mL excess output is the concerning finding
options.D: All three hours are within normal parameters

questions.rationale: Severe malabsorption requiring intervention Rationale: Low albumin, anemia, and vitamin deficiencies indicate significant malabsorption requiring nutritional support.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the ethical and legal dimensions of care for a client with a perforated peptic ulcer who requires emergency surgery but refuses the procedure based on religious beliefs. The nurse's most appropriate action reflects:

options.A: Override the refusal — the client will die without surgery
options.B: Assess the client's decision-making capacity; if competent, a client has the legal and ethical right to refuse treatment even if life-threatening. The nurse should ensure the client is fully informed of the consequences, document the informed refusal, notify the provider and ethics team, and provide compassionate palliative/comfort care while respecting the client's autonomy
options.C: Obtain a court order to proceed with surgery without consent
options.D: Ask the family to convince the client to accept surgery

questions.rationale: Expected initial output—becomes thicker over time Rationale: Initial ileostomy output is loose, dark green, and may contain blood. It becomes thicker and more yellow-brown as adaptation occurs.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease is being discharged on vedolizumab (a gut-selective antiintegrin biologic). The nurse correctly teaches the client that vedolizumab differs from anti-TNF agents because:

options.A: Vedolizumab is more toxic than anti-TNF agents
options.B: Vedolizumab selectively blocks lymphocyte trafficking to the gut (intestinal-selective) without broadly suppressing systemic immunity — this means lower risk of systemic infections (including TB reactivation) compared to anti-TNF agents, though GI infections and progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) remain rare concerns
options.C: Vedolizumab is an antibiotic that targets Crohn's-specific bacteria
options.D: Vedolizumab has the same mechanism as infliximab

questions.rationale: Days to weeks—output becomes thicker, yellow-brown Rationale: The small bowel adapts over time, and output consistency changes from liquid to thicker paste.

questions.text: The nurse is conducting a quality improvement audit on TPN-related complications across the unit over the past quarter. The data show 4 CLABSI events, 3 episodes of severe hyperglycemia (>400 mg/dL), 2 cases of refeeding syndrome, and 1 case of air embolism. Which system-level interventions should the quality team prioritize? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Implement a TPN bundle checklist: daily line necessity review, strict aseptic technique protocol, daily blood glucose monitoring schedule
options.B: Mandate education on refeeding syndrome prevention: start TPN at 50% of goal rate and advance gradually in malnourished clients
options.C: Review air embolism prevention protocol: clamp catheters during tubing changes, use Luer-lock connections, position client in Trendelenburg during line changes
options.D: Discontinue TPN use across the unit to eliminate risks
options.E: Conduct simulation training for all staff on TPN complication recognition and response

questions.rationale: Red/moist stoma, Output occurring, Slight bleeding, Intact skin Rationale: These indicate proper function. A dusky purple stoma (D) indicates ischemia and is NOT normal.

questions.text: A client presents with a 3-week history of right-sided flank pain, fever, and pneumaturia (bubbles in the urine). He has a 15-year history of Crohn's disease. The nurse's clinical hypothesis is:

options.A: Kidney stones passing through the ureter
options.B: Enterovesical fistula from Crohn's disease — bowel-to-bladder fistula allows intestinal gas and bacteria to enter the bladder, causing pneumaturia and polymicrobial UTIs. This is pathognomonic and requires urgent provider notification, CT imaging, and surgical consultation
options.C: Prostatitis causing urinary symptoms with referred flank pain
options.D: UTI with gas-producing organisms unrelated to Crohn's disease

questions.rationale: Hypokalemia and dehydration Rationale: High-volume ileostomy output causes significant fluid and potassium losses, requiring monitoring and replacement.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a client with GERD who has developed iron deficiency anemia. The nurse correctly explains the pathophysiological link:

options.A: GERD directly destroys red blood cells
options.B: Chronic acid exposure to the esophageal mucosa causes mucosal erosions and microhemorrhage (occult bleeding), leading to chronic iron loss and eventually iron deficiency anemia. Additionally, long-term PPI use impairs iron absorption by reducing gastric acid needed to convert dietary iron to its absorbable ferrous (Fe²⁺) form
options.C: GERD prevents the absorption of vitamin B12, causing anemia
options.D: Antacids bind dietary iron, preventing absorption

questions.rationale: Liquid passing around impaction (overflow) Rationale: Small amounts of liquid stool may pass around an obstruction, mistakenly suggesting improvement.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with meningitis, SIADH, and hyponatremia (Na⁺ 115 mEq/L) being treated with 3% hypertonic saline. After 6 hours, the sodium has risen to 132 mEq/L (a correction of 17 mEq/L). The nurse's priority action is:

options.A: Continue the infusion — the sodium needs to normalize completely
options.B: Stop the hypertonic saline infusion immediately and notify the provider — sodium was corrected by 17 mEq/L in 6 hours, which far exceeds the safe maximum of 8–12 mEq/L per 24 hours. Overcorrection risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/CPM), causing irreversible brainstem destruction
options.C: Increase the rate to capitalize on the response
options.D: Add free water IV to slow the correction

questions.rationale: May mask peritonitis/perforation signs Rationale: Pain medications can mask peritoneal signs that indicate need for emergency surgery.

questions.text: A client with peritonitis following bowel perforation is on broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + metronidazole). On day 7, the client develops new-onset profuse watery diarrhea with a distinctive foul odor. WBC rises to 22,000 from 14,000. The nurse's clinical judgment identifies:

options.A: The peritonitis has resolved and bowel function is returning
options.B: Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) colitis superinfection — broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupt normal colonic flora, allowing C. diff overgrowth. The nurse must immediately implement contact precautions, obtain stool for C. diff toxin assay, notify the provider, and anticipate antibiotic change (oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin) while discontinuing the offending broad-spectrum antibiotics if safe to do so
options.C: The antibiotic is causing antibiotic-associated diarrhea that will resolve on its own
options.D: The client needs a fleet enema to clear the infectious diarrhea

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Constant pain, Fever, Elevated WBC, Hemodynamic instability, Absent sounds Rationale: These indicate strangulation with tissue ischemia. Intermittent cramping (D) is typical of simple obstruction.

questions.text: A client with cholecystitis has undergone ERCP for common bile duct stone removal. Postprocedure, the client develops new-onset epigastric pain radiating to the back, elevated lipase (680 U/L), and elevated amylase (520 U/L). The nurse recognizes:

options.A: These are expected post-ERCP findings that do not require intervention
options.B: Post-ERCP pancreatitis — the endoscopic manipulation of the pancreatic/biliary ducts can trigger pancreatic enzyme activation. The nurse must notify the provider, ensure NPO status, initiate aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (the cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management), and monitor for respiratory, renal, and hemodynamic complications
options.C: The ERCP successfully resolved the stone — these are normal post-procedural values
options.D: The client has developed a bowel perforation from the endoscope

questions.rationale: Complete absence of stool AND flatus Rationale: Obstipation is the complete inability to pass stool or gas, indicating complete obstruction.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing care for a client admitted with shingles who is also on warfarin for atrial fibrillation. Valacyclovir is prescribed. The nurse identifies which important drug interaction:

options.A: Valacyclovir directly neutralizes the anticoagulant effect of warfarin
options.B: Valacyclovir can increase the anticoagulant effect of warfarin, increasing bleeding risk. INR levels must be monitored more closely during antiviral therapy, and the provider must be notified of the concurrent therapy
options.C: Warfarin prevents the antiviral mechanism of valacyclovir
options.D: There is no clinically significant interaction between valacyclovir and warfarin

questions.rationale: Meningococcemia with DIC—emergency Rationale: Rapidly spreading petechiae/purpura indicates meningococcal septicemia with DIC—immediately life-threatening.

questions.text: The nurse is providing care for a client with a high-output ileostomy following UC colectomy. The client's output is 2,400 mL/day. The nurse performs a complete assessment. Which complication cluster is the nurse most concerned about? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Dehydration and pre-renal acute kidney injury from volume depletion
options.B: Hyponatremia and hypokalemia from electrolyte-rich stomal losses
options.C: Vitamin B12 deficiency from terminal ileum removal
options.D: Nephrolithiasis (kidney stones) — dehydration + increased oxalate absorption postileostomy increases stone risk
options.E: Hypernatremia from excessive sodium retention

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Petechiae, Oozing, Mottled extremities, Prolonged PT/PTT, Low fibrinogen Rationale: DIC causes both bleeding (consumption of clotting factors) and thrombosis. Platelets are LOW, not elevated.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing a full medication profile for a client with IBD. The client is on: mesalamine, prednisone (tapering), azathioprine, calcium + vitamin D supplement, omeprazole, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX). The TMP-SMX was just added. The nurse identifies a critical interaction:

options.A: Omeprazole and mesalamine interact to cause liver toxicity
options.B: Azathioprine and TMP-SMX together cause additive bone marrow suppression — both are myelosuppressive agents; their combination significantly increases the risk of leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and life-threatening infection. The nurse must notify the provider before administering
options.C: Calcium supplements block prednisone absorption
options.D: Mesalamine and omeprazole cannot be given on the same day

questions.rationale: Microvascular thrombosis from DIC Rationale: DIC causes small vessel clotting, leading to tissue ischemia and gangrene in extremities.

questions.text: A client with BPH is being evaluated for urinary retention with a post-void residual (PVR) of 420 mL on bladder scan. The client has been on prazosin for 6 months. The nurse identifies which additional assessment finding would indicate the BPH has caused upper urinary tract involvement (hydronephrosis/renal damage)?

options.A: Urgency and nocturia
options.B: Elevated BUN and creatinine with bilateral flank pain and hydronephrosis on ultrasound — prolonged urinary retention increases back-pressure on the ureters and kidneys, causing bilateral hydronephrosis and obstructive nephropathy
options.C: A PSA level of 4.0 ng/mL
options.D: International Prostate Symptom Score of 22

questions.rationale: 24 hours Rationale: Meningococcal meningitis can cause death within 24 hours—it is a true medical emergency.

questions.text: A client with severe Crohn's disease, malnutrition (albumin 1.8 g/dL), and an enterocutaneous fistula is being started on TPN. The dietitian calculates caloric and protein goals. When initiating TPN in a severely malnourished client, the nurse understands which critical principle must guide the initiation plan?

options.A: Start at full goal rate immediately to correct malnutrition as rapidly as possible
options.B: Start TPN at 50% of the goal rate and advance gradually over 2–3 days to prevent refeeding syndrome — severely malnourished clients have depleted intracellular phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. Rapid glucose delivery drives insulin release and massive intracellular shifts of these electrolytes, causing life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias
options.C: Start TPN at 150% of goal to aggressively correct the albumin deficit
options.D: Begin oral feeds first and only add TPN if the client cannot consume 100% of goal by mouth

questions.rationale: Postherpetic neuralgia Rationale: PHN is defined as pain persisting more than 90 days after the acute shingles rash has healed.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the complete clinical picture of a client with known Crohn's disease who presents with: decreased vision in the right eye with a painful red eye, arthritis in multiple joints flaring simultaneously, raised tender red nodules on both shins, and rectal bleeding. The nurse recognizes these as:

options.A: Unrelated comorbidities that happened to occur simultaneously
options.B: Extraintestinal manifestations of Crohn's disease: anterior uveitis (eye), peripheral arthropathy, erythema nodosum (shin nodules), and rectal bleeding from active gut disease — all occur simultaneously with intestinal flares and reflect the systemic inflammatory nature of IBD. Urgent ophthalmology referral is needed to prevent vision loss from uveitis
options.C: Adverse effects of mesalamine therapy
options.D: Signs of Crohn's disease-related sepsis involving multiple organ systems

questions.rationale: A, B, C, E, F — Gabapentin, Pregabalin, TCAs, Lidocaine patches, Capsaicin Rationale: Multiple medications target neuropathic pain. Antibiotics (D) are not indicated for PHN.

questions.text: A client with peptic ulcer disease has completed H. pylori eradication therapy. The nurse explains the correct test of cure process:

options.A: The urea breath test or stool antigen test should be performed at least 4 weeks after completing all antibiotics AND 2 weeks after stopping PPIs — testing too early or while still on PPIs causes false negative results, giving false reassurance that eradication succeeded
options.B: A repeat EGD is the only accurate method to confirm H. pylori eradication
options.C: The absence of symptoms is sufficient confirmation of eradication
options.D: Serological (blood) antibody testing is the preferred test of cure after treatment

questions.rationale: A, B, C, D, F — Age >50, Severe acute pain, Delayed treatment, Extensive rash, Immunocompromised Rationale: These increase PHN risk. Young age (E) is protective, not a risk factor.

questions.text: The nurse is providing care for a client who had a right hemicolectomy and developed a prolonged ileus. On day 7 post-op, the abdomen remains distended, the client has not passed flatus, bowel sounds are absent, and NG output is 800 mL in 24 hours of bilious material. The provider is considering a neostigmine challenge. The nurse's understanding of neostigmine in this context is:

options.A: Neostigmine is an antibiotic used for ileus from infection
options.B: Neostigmine is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that increases acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, enhancing GI motility. It is used for colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome) and refractory ileus. The nurse must monitor closely for bradycardia and bronchospasm — atropine must be immediately available as an antidote
options.C: Neostigmine reduces gastric acid secretion to treat the ileus
options.D: Neostigmine is used only for myasthenia gravis and is not indicated here

questions.rationale: 300-500 mL of greenish-brown bile Rationale: Normal T-tube output is 300-500 mL/day of greenish-brown bile. This decreases as bile begins flowing into duodenum.

questions.text: The nurse receives a critical lab value for a client with active PUD bleeding: INR 4.8 (therapeutic for anticoagulation is 2–3; goal here is normal < 1.3). The client is on warfarin. The nurse anticipates which immediate orders? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Hold warfarin
options.B: Administer vitamin K (phytonadione) IV or oral per provider order
options.C: Administer fresh frozen plasma (FFP) for rapid reversal if bleeding is severe

questions.rationale: Normal progression—bile flowing into duodenum Rationale: Decreasing T-tube drainage indicates bile is now flowing through the common bile duct into the intestine.
